The results demonstrate high accuracy, efficiency, and reliable reuse, providing a scalable foundation for autonomous task-driven soft robot design.

</details>


### [329] [Real-Time Structural Detection for Indoor Navigation from 3D LiDAR Using Bird's-Eye-View Images](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.19830)
*Guanliang Li,Pedro Espinosa Angulo,David Perez Saura,Santiago Tapia Fernandez*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The paper introduces a BEV-based framework for real-time, lightweight structural detection using 3D LiDAR on resource-limited robots. It systematically evaluates feature extraction strategies and highlights YOLO-OBB as the best compromise between robustness and speed.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Efficient, lightweight structural perception is a critical need for mapping and autonomous navigation on robots without high computational resources. Existing 3D and 2D methods are either too computationally intensive or insufficiently robust, motivating a new solution.

Method: The proposed method converts 3D LiDAR data into 2D BEV images for efficient processing. Several feature extraction strategies, including classical geometric methods and a deep learning YOLO-OBB detector, are evaluated. A spatiotemporal fusion module is added for enhanced stability.

Result: Classical methods like Hough and LSD are fast but struggle with noise and fragmentation. RANSAC is more robust but too slow for real-time applications. YOLO-OBB strikes the best balance, achieving 10 Hz performance on a low-power SBC, even without GPU support.

Conclusion: The paper showcases a novel BEV-based perception pipeline that is both computationally efficient and effective, enabling reliable use of 3D LiDAR for real-time navigation on resource-constrained robotic systems.

Abstract: Efficient structural perception is essential for mapping and autonomous navigation on resource-constrained robots. Existing 3D methods are computationally prohibitive, while traditional 2D geometric approaches lack robustness. This paper presents a lightweight, real-time framework that projects 3D LiDAR data into 2D Bird's-Eye-View (BEV) images to enable efficient detection of structural elements relevant to mapping and navigation. Within this representation, we systematically evaluate several feature extraction strategies, including classical geometric techniques (Hough Transform, RANSAC, and LSD) and a deep learning detector based on YOLO-OBB. The resulting detections are integrated through a spatiotemporal fusion module that improves stability and robustness across consecutive frames. Experiments conducted on a standard mobile robotic platform highlight clear performance trade-offs. Classical methods such as Hough and LSD provide fast responses but exhibit strong sensitivity to noise, with LSD producing excessive segment fragmentation that leads to system congestion. RANSAC offers improved robustness but fails to meet real-time constraints. In contrast, the YOLO-OBB-based approach achieves the best balance between robustness and computational efficiency, maintaining an end-to-end latency (satisfying 10 Hz operation) while effectively filtering cluttered observations in a low-power single-board computer (SBC) without using GPU acceleration. The main contribution of this work is a computationally efficient BEV-based perception pipeline enabling reliable real-time structural detection from 3D LiDAR on resource-constrained robotic platforms that cannot rely on GPU-intensive processing.

</details>


### [330] [Multi-Agent Motion Planning on Industrial Magnetic Levitation Platforms: A Hybrid ADMM-HOCBF approach](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.19838)
*Bavo Tistaert,Stan Servaes,Alejandro Gonzalez-Garcia,Ibrahim Ibrahim,Louis Callens,Jan Swevers,Wilm Decré*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The study introduces a hybrid decentralized MPC method using ADMM and HOCBF for multi-agent systems, validated with simulations and real-world deployment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the scalability issues and safety challenges in classical centralized MPC for holonomic multi-agent systems.

Method: Combines decentralized ADMM with centralized HOCBF architecture and validates it using simulations and real-world experiments.

Result: Improved scalability compared to centralized MPC, with real-time execution on an industrial platform.

Conclusion: The proposed framework balances scalability, safety, and real-time capability for holonomic multi-agent systems.

Abstract: This paper presents a novel hybrid motion planning method for holonomic multi-agent systems. The proposed decentralised model predictive control (MPC) framework tackles the intractability of classical centralised MPC for a growing number of agents while providing safety guarantees. This is achieved by combining a decentralised version of the alternating direction method of multipliers (ADMM) with a centralised high-order control barrier function (HOCBF) architecture. Simulation results show significant improvement in scalability over classical centralised MPC. We validate the efficacy and real-time capability of the proposed method by developing a highly efficient C++ implementation and deploying the resulting trajectories on a real industrial magnetic levitation platform.

</details>


### [331] [Beyond detection: cooperative multi-agent reasoning for rapid onboard EO crisis response](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.19858)
*Alejandro D. Mousist,Pedro Delgado de Robles Martín,Raquel Lladró Climent,Julian Cobos Aparicio*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The paper proposes a multi-agent system for onboard Earth observation processing to quickly identify hazardous events while addressing resource and bandwidth constraints.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation stems from the need for faster disaster response enabled by Earth Observation missions as existing systems suffer from delays caused by ground-centric processing and data bottlenecks.

Method: The method introduces hierarchical multi-agent architecture using AI agents for distributed, event-driven decision-making directly on satellites, combining various models for multimodal data analysis.

Result: The system was validated on wildfire and flood scenarios, showing reduced computational load and coherent decision-making, proving feasibility on a current satellite platform.

Conclusion: This architecture demonstrates that distributed agent-based reasoning can improve efficiency and decision-making for autonomous disaster monitoring in future satellite constellations.

Abstract: Rapid identification of hazardous events is essential for next-generation Earth Observation (EO) missions supporting disaster response. However, current monitoring pipelines remain largely ground-centric, introducing latency due to downlink limitations, multi-source data fusion constraints, and the computational cost of exhaustive scene analysis.
  This work proposes a hierarchical multi-agent architecture for onboard EO processing under strict resource and bandwidth constraints. The system enables the exploitation of complementary multimodal observations by coordinating specialized AI agents within an event-driven decision pipeline. AI agents can be deployed across multiple nodes in a distributed setting, such as satellite platforms. An Early Warning agent generates fast hypotheses from onboard observations and selectively activates domain-specific analysis agents, while a Decision agent consolidates the evidence to issue a final alert. The architecture combines vision-language models, traditional remote sensing analysis tools, and role-specialized agents to enable structured reasoning over multimodal observations while minimizing unnecessary computation.
  A proof-of-concept implementation was executed on the engineering model of an edge-computing platform currently deployed in orbit, using representative satellite data. Experiments on wildfire and flood monitoring scenarios show that the proposed routing-based pipeline significantly reduces computational overhead while maintaining coherent decision outputs, demonstrating the feasibility of distributed agent-based reasoning for future autonomous EO constellations.

</details>


### [332] [Radar-Inertial Odometry with Online Spatio-Temporal Calibration via Continuous-Time IMU Modeling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.19958)
*Vlaho-Josip Štironja,Luka Petrović,Juraj Peršić,Ivan Marković,Ivan Petrović*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper introduces a Radar-Inertial Odometry framework addressing joint spatial and temporal calibration using continuous-time modeling with B-splines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing RIO systems struggle with unknown radar-IMU calibration and temporal misalignment under adverse conditions.

Method: A factor-graph optimization framework with continuous-time modeling via uniform cubic B-splines is developed for online spatial and temporal calibration.

Result: The framework reliably converges on temporal offset and extrinsic calibration parameters without environment-specific dependencies.

Conclusion: The proposed system improves robustness for radar-inertial odometry in challenging conditions, handling sensor misalignment effectively.

Abstract: Radar-Inertial Odometry (RIO) has emerged as a robust alternative to vision- and LiDAR-based odometry in challenging conditions such as low light, fog, featureless environments, or in adverse weather. However, many existing RIO approaches assume known radar-IMU extrinsic calibration or rely on sufficient motion excitation for online extrinsic estimation, while temporal misalignment between sensors is often neglected or treated independently. In this work, we present a RIO framework that performs joint online spatial and temporal calibration within a factor-graph optimization formulation, based on continuous-time modeling of inertial measurements using uniform cubic B-splines. The proposed continuous-time representation of acceleration and angular velocity accurately captures the asynchronous nature of radar-IMU measurements, enabling reliable convergence of both the temporal offset and extrinsic calibration parameters, without relying on scan matching, target tracking, or environment-specific assumptions.

</details>


### [333] [GustPilot: A Hierarchical DRL-INDI Framework for Wind-Resilient Quadrotor Navigation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.19966)
*Amir Atef Habel,Roohan Ahmed Khan,Fawad Mehboob,Clement Fortin,Dzmitry Tsetserukou*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper presents GustPilot, a wind-resilient navigation system for lightweight quadrotors, featuring a DRL policy for planning and an INDI controller for fast disturbance rejection, demonstrating superior performance in challenging conditions compared to baseline methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation behind this work is to address the challenge of wind disturbances, which destabilize lightweight quadrotors during autonomous navigation, posing significant barriers to their reliable operation.

Method: The paper proposes a hierarchical stack called GustPilot, combining Deep Reinforcement Learning (DRL) for high-level velocity planning with a geometric Incremental Nonlinear Dynamic Inversion (INDI) controller for low-level tracking that rejects wind-induced disturbances. Training involves fan-jet domain randomization to enhance robustness.

Result: The system, tested on real quadrotors, achieved a 94.7% success rate across scenarios compared to 55.0% for the baseline. It demonstrated 50% reduced tracking error and sustained speeds of 1.34 m/s under 3.5 m/s wind disturbances, generalizing well to more complex scenarios without retraining.

Conclusion: The results highlight that the combination of DRL for planning and INDI for disturbance rejection offers a robust and versatile solution, significantly advancing wind-resilient autonomous navigation for quadrotors.

Abstract: Wind disturbances remain a key barrier to reliable autonomous navigation for lightweight quadrotors, where the rapidly varying airflow can destabilize both planning and tracking. This paper introduces GustPilot, a hierarchical wind-resilient navigation stack in which a deep reinforcement learning (DRL) policy generates inertial-frame velocity reference for gate traversal. At the same time, a geometric Incremental Nonlinear Dynamic Inversion (INDI) controller provides low-level tracking with fast residual disturbance rejection. The INDI layer achieves this by providing incremental feedback on both specific linear acceleration and angular acceleration rate, using onboard sensor measurements to reject wind disturbances rapidly. Robustness is obtained through a two-level strategy, wind-aware planning learned via fan-jet domain randomization during training, and rapid execution-time disturbance rejection by the INDI tracking controller. We evaluate GustPilot in real flights on a 50g quad-copter platform against a DRL-PID baseline across four scenarios ranging from no-wind to fully dynamic conditions with a moving gate and a moving disturbance source. Despite being trained only in a minimal single-gate and single-fan setup, the policy generalizes to significantly more complex environments (up to six gates and four fans) without retraining. Across 80 experiments, DRL-INDI achieves a 94.7% versus 55.0% for DRL-PID as average Overall Success Rate (OSR), reduces tracking RMSE up to 50%, and sustains speeds up to 1.34 m/s under wind disturbances up to 3.5 m/s. These results demonstrate that combining DRL-based velocity planning with structured INDI disturbance rejection provides a practical and generalizable approach to wind-resilient autonomous flight navigation.

</details>


### [334] [Uncertainty Matters: Structured Probabilistic Online Mapping for Motion Prediction in Autonomous Driving](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.20076)
*Pritom Gogoi,Faris Janjoš,Bin Yang,Andreas Look*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The paper proposes a probabilistic method with structured uncertainty modeling for online map generation, achieving state-of-the-art results in map-based motion prediction.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Modern mapping models lack uncertainty estimation and fail to capture spatial correlations in road geometry due to simplified assumptions.

Method: Introduces a Low-Rank plus Diagonal (LRPD) covariance decomposition to model intra-element dependencies and spatial correlations in road geometry.

Result: Demonstrated improved map generation quality and state-of-the-art performance in map-based motion prediction on the nuScenes dataset.

Conclusion: Accounting for uncertainty in online map generation significantly benefits autonomous driving planning and perception tasks.

Abstract: Online map generation and trajectory prediction are critical components of the autonomous driving perception-prediction-planning pipeline. While modern vectorized mapping models achieve high geometric accuracy, they typically treat map estimation as a deterministic task, discarding structural uncertainty. Existing probabilistic approaches often rely on diagonal covariance matrices, which assume independence between points and fail to capture the strong spatial correlations inherent in road geometry. To address this, we propose a structured probabilistic formulation for online map generation. Our method explicitly models intra-element dependencies by predicting a dense covariance matrix, parameterized via a Low-Rank plus Diagonal (LRPD) covariance decomposition. This formulation represents uncertainty as a combination of a low-rank component, which captures global spatial structure, and a diagonal component representing independent local noise, thereby capturing geometric correlations without the prohibitive computational cost of full covariance matrices. Evaluations on the nuScenes dataset demonstrate that our uncertainty-aware framework yields consistent improvements in online map generation quality compared to deterministic baselines. Furthermore, our approach establishes new state-of-the-art performance for map-based motion prediction, highlighting the critical role of uncertainty in planning tasks. Code is published under link-available-soon.

</details>


### [335] [Not an Obstacle for Dog, but a Hazard for Human: A Co-Ego Navigation System for Guide Dog Robots](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.20121)
*Ruiping Liu,Jingqi Zhang,Junwei Zheng,Yufan Chen,Peter Seungjune Lee,Di Wen,Kunyu Peng,Jiaming Zhang,Kailun Yang,Katja Mombaur,Rainer Stiefelhagen*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This study introduces a robotic guide dog system, Co-Ego, that combines ground-level sensing and elevated egocentric perspective to address navigation hazards overlooked by previous quadruped robot systems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The limited availability of guide dogs for Blind and Low-Vision (BLV) individuals leaves many without access, and current robotic alternatives fail to detect obstacles from a human's elevated viewpoint, creating safety risks.

Method: The Co-Ego system integrates ground-level sensors on a quadruped robot with an elevated egocentric perspective of the user, adopting a dual-branch obstacle avoidance framework.

Result: A controlled user study with blindfolded sighted participants demonstrated that the system's cross-view sensor fusion significantly reduced collision times and cognitive load compared to unassisted or single-view systems.

Conclusion: Addressing viewpoint asymmetry through cross-view sensor integration is essential for safe and effective robotic guide dog navigation.

Abstract: Guide dogs offer independence to Blind and Low-Vision (BLV) individuals, yet their limited availability leaves the vast majority of BLV users without access. Quadruped robotic guide dogs present a promising alternative, but existing systems rely solely on the robot's ground-level sensors for navigation, overlooking a critical class of hazards: obstacles that are transparent to the robot yet dangerous at human body height, such as bent branches. We term this the viewpoint asymmetry problem and present the first system to explicitly address it. Our Co-Ego system adopts a dual-branch obstacle avoidance framework that integrates the robot-centric ground sensing with the user's elevated egocentric perspective to ensure comprehensive navigation safety. Deployed on a quadruped robot, the system is evaluated in a controlled user study with sighted participants under blindfold across three conditions: unassisted, single-view, and cross-view fusion. Results demonstrate that cross-view fusion significantly reduces collision times and cognitive load, verifying the necessity of viewpoint complementarity for safe robotic guide dog navigation.

</details>


### [336] [KUKAloha: A General, Low-Cost, and Shared-Control based Teleoperation Framework for Construction Robot Arm](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.20129)
*Yifan Xu,Qizhang Shen,Vineet Kamat,Carol Menassa*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The paper introduces KUKAloha, a low-cost teleoperation framework for construction robot arms, improving safety, efficiency, and precise operation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The authors aim to address challenges in controlling large-scale construction robot arms, focusing on improving safety, precision, and reducing operator workload.

Method: The framework uses a leader-follower approach with a lightweight leading arm for human guidance and an AprilTag-based perception module for precise tasks.

Result: KUKAloha, implemented on a KUKA arm, showed decreased operator workload, better task efficiency, and practical viability in usability trials.

Conclusion: KUKAloha presents a scalable, shared-control solution that enhances human-robot collaboration in construction scenarios.

Abstract: This paper presents KUKAloha, a general, low-cost, and shared-control teleoperation framework designed for construction robot arms. The proposed system employs a leader-follower paradigm in which a lightweight leading arm enables intuitive human guidance for coarse robot motion, while an autonomous perception module based on AprilTag detection performs precise alignment and grasp execution. By explicitly decoupling human control from fine manipulation, KUKAloha improves safety and repeatability when operating large-scale manipulators. We implement the framework on a KUKA robot arm and conduct a usability study with representative construction manipulation tasks. Experimental results demonstrate that KUKAloha reduces operator workload, improves task completion efficiency, and provides a practical solution for scalable demonstration collection and shared human-robot control in construction environments.

</details>


### [337] [AGILE: A Comprehensive Workflow for Humanoid Loco-Manipulation Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.20147)
*Huihua Zhao,Rafael Cathomen,Lionel Gulich,Wei Liu,Efe Arda Ongan,Michael Lin,Shalin Jain,Soha Pouya,Yan Chang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The paper introduces AGILE, a standardized, end-to-end workflow for humanoid reinforcement learning (RL), addressing the challenges of sim-to-real transfer by integrating stages like environment verification, training, evaluation, and deployment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The main motivation is to overcome the lack of systematic infrastructure in humanoid RL that connects environment verification, training, evaluation, and deployment, leading to improved sim-to-real transfer reliability.

Method: AGILE consists of four stages: interactive environment verification, reproducible training with stabilizations, a unified evaluation system, and descriptor-driven deployment. The workflow also includes tools for automated regression testing and robustness assessment.

Result: The AGILE workflow was validated on five humanoid skills across two platforms (Unitree G1 and Booster T1) and achieved consistent sim-to-real transfer.

Conclusion: AGILE demonstrates that a standardized workflow improves the reproducibility and reliability of humanoid RL development, addressing common failure modes in sim-to-real scenarios.

Abstract: Recent advances in reinforcement learning (RL) have enabled impressive humanoid behaviors in simulation, yet transferring these results to new robots remains challenging. In many real deployments, the primary bottleneck is no longer simulation throughput or algorithm design, but the absence of systematic infrastructure that links environment verification, training, evaluation, and deployment in a coherent loop.
  To address this gap, we present AGILE, an end-to-end workflow for humanoid RL that standardizes the policy-development lifecycle to mitigate common sim-to-real failure modes. AGILE comprises four stages: (1) interactive environment verification, (2) reproducible training, (3) unified evaluation, and (4) descriptor-driven deployment via robot/task configuration descriptors. For evaluation stage, AGILE supports both scenario-based tests and randomized rollouts under a shared suite of motion-quality diagnostics, enabling automated regression testing and principled robustness assessment. AGILE also incorporates a set of training stabilizations and algorithmic enhancements in training stage to improve optimization stability and sim-to-real transfer.
  With this pipeline in place, we validate AGILE across five representative humanoid skills spanning locomotion, recovery, motion imitation, and loco-manipulation on two hardware platforms (Unitree G1 and Booster T1), achieving consistent sim-to-real transfer. Overall, AGILE shows that a standardized, end-to-end workflow can substantially improve the reliability and reproducibility of humanoid RL development.

</details>


### [338] [HortiMulti: A Multi-Sensor Dataset for Localisation and Mapping in Horticultural Polytunnels](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.20150)
*Shuoyuan Xu,Zhipeng Zhong,Tiago Barros,Matthew Coombes,Cristiano Premebida,Hao Wu,Cunjia Liu*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The paper introduces HortiMulti, a comprehensive multimodal and cross-season dataset for robotic research in challenging horticultural polytunnel environments, addressing gaps in available datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation stems from the lack of sufficient real-world datasets for autonomous robotic tasks in horticultural environments, which are crucial given the economic importance of this domain.

Method: The authors created the HortiMulti dataset by collecting multimodal data (LiDAR, RGB cameras, IMU, GNSS, and wheel odometry) in polytunnels during a full growing season, providing ground truth trajectories and supporting benchmarks for evaluation.

Result: HortiMulti highlights significant shortcomings in state-of-the-art SLAM algorithms for horticulture, showcasing the need for improved perception systems for such environments.

Conclusion: HortiMulti serves as an essential resource for advancing robotic perception systems, offering a standardized platform for testing and developing methods suitable for complex horticultural environments.

Abstract: Agricultural robotics is gaining increasing relevance in both research and real-world deployment. As these systems are expected to operate autonomously in more complex tasks, the availability of representative real-world datasets becomes essential. While domains such as urban and forestry robotics benefit from large and established benchmarks, horticultural environments remain comparatively under-explored despite the economic significance of this sector. To address this gap, we present HortiMulti, a multimodal, cross-season dataset collected in commercial strawberry and raspberry polytunnels across an entire growing season, capturing substantial appearance variation, dynamic foliage, specular reflections from plastic covers, severe perceptual aliasing, and GNSS-unreliable conditions, all of which directly degrade existing localisation and perception algorithms. The sensor suite includes two 3D LiDARs, four RGB cameras, an IMU, GNSS, and wheel odometry. Ground truth trajectories are derived from a combination of Total Station surveying, AprilTag fiducial markers, and LiDAR-inertial odometry, spanning dense, sparse, and marker-free coverage to support evaluation under both controlled and realistic conditions. We release time-synchronised raw measurements, calibration files, reference trajectories, and baseline benchmarks for visual, LiDAR, and multi-sensor SLAM, with results confirming that current state-of-the-art methods remain inadequate for reliable polytunnel deployment, establishing HortiMulti as a one-stop resource for developing and testing robotic perception systems in horticulture environments.

</details>


### [339] [The Robot's Inner Critic: Self-Refinement of Social Behaviors through VLM-based Replanning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.20164)
*Jiyu Lim,Youngwoo Yoon,Kwanghyun Park*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper introduces CRISP, an autonomous framework for robots to enhance social behavior through critique and replanning using Vision-Language Models (VLM).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The study addresses the lack of flexibility and autonomy in conventional robot social behavior generation, which often relies on predefined motions or human intervention.

Method: CRISP employs a framework involving robot joint extraction, context-based behavior planning, low-level joint control, VLM-based social appropriateness evaluation, and iterative refinement through reward-based search.

Result: A user study demonstrated that CRISP achieved higher preference and situational appropriateness ratings across five robot types and 20 scenarios compared to prior methods.

Conclusion: CRISP enhances autonomous robot interaction, offering improved social behavior generation while minimizing human intervention and supporting cross-platform applications.

Abstract: Conventional robot social behavior generation has been limited in flexibility and autonomy, relying on predefined motions or human feedback. This study proposes CRISP (Critique-and-Replan for Interactive Social Presence), an autonomous framework where a robot critiques and replans its own actions by leveraging a Vision-Language Model (VLM) as a `human-like social critic.' CRISP integrates (1) extraction of movable joints and constraints by analyzing the robot's description file (e.g., MJCF), (2) generation of step-by-step behavior plans based on situational context, (3) generation of low-level joint control code by referencing visual information (joint range-of-motion visualizations), (4) VLM-based evaluation of social appropriateness and naturalness, including pinpointing erroneous steps, and (5) iterative refinement of behaviors through reward-based search. This approach is not tied to a specific robot API; it can generate subtly different, human-like motions on various platforms using only the robot's structure file. In a user study involving five different robot types and 20 scenarios, including mobile manipulators and humanoids, our proposed method achieved significantly higher preference and situational appropriateness ratings compared to previous methods. This research presents a general framework that minimizes human intervention while expanding the robot's autonomous interaction capabilities and cross-platform applicability.
  Detailed result videos and supplementary information regarding this work are available at: https://limjiyu99.github.io/inner-critic/

</details>


### [340] [IndoorR2X: Indoor Robot-to-Everything Coordination with LLM-Driven Planning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.20182)
*Fan Yang,Soumya Teotia,Shaunak A. Mehta,Prajit KrisshnaKumar,Quanting Xie,Jun Liu,Yueqi Song,Li Wenkai,Atsunori Moteki,Kanji Uchino,Yonatan Bisk*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The paper introduces IndoorR2X, a benchmark and simulation framework for multi-robot task planning using Robot-to-Everything (R2X) communication and IoT sensor integration to enhance scene understanding and task efficiency in indoor environments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the limitations of Robot-to-Robot (R2R) communication in overcoming partial observability in indoor scene understanding without requiring extensive exploration or scaling robot team sizes, and to leverage IoT sensors already present in many indoor environments.

Method: The framework combines observations from mobile robots and static IoT sensors to create a global semantic state, uses Large Language Model (LLM)-based planning for high-level coordination, and provides configurable simulations to evaluate semantic coordination strategies.

Result: Experiments demonstrate that IoT-augmented modeling improves the efficiency and reliability of multi-robot systems, alongside identifying important insights and failure modes for collaboration between robots and IoT sensors.

Conclusion: IoT sensors play a critical role in enhancing multi-robot scene understanding through improved semantic modeling and planning, offering increased efficiency and scalability for indoor task coordination frameworks like IndoorR2X.

Abstract: Although robot-to-robot (R2R) communication improves indoor scene understanding beyond what a single robot can achieve, R2R alone cannot overcome partial observability without substantial exploration overhead or scaling team size. In contrast, many indoor environments already include low-cost Internet of Things (IoT) sensors (e.g., cameras) that provide persistent, building-wide context beyond onboard perception. We therefore introduce IndoorR2X, the first benchmark and simulation framework for Large Language Model (LLM)-driven multi-robot task planning with Robot-to-Everything (R2X) perception and communication in indoor environments. IndoorR2X integrates observations from mobile robots and static IoT devices to construct a global semantic state that supports scalable scene understanding, reduces redundant exploration, and enables high-level coordination through LLM-based planning. IndoorR2X provides configurable simulation environments, sensor layouts, robot teams, and task suites to systematically evaluate high-level semantic coordination strategies. Extensive experiments across diverse settings demonstrate that IoT-augmented world modeling improves multi-robot efficiency and reliability, and we highlight key insights and failure modes for advancing LLM-based collaboration between robot teams and indoor IoT sensors.

</details>


<div id='cs.SE'></div>

# cs.SE [[Back]](#toc)

### [341] [Goedel-Code-Prover: Hierarchical Proof Search for Open State-of-the-Art Code Verification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.19329)
*Zenan Li,Ziran Yang,Deyuan,He,Haoyu Zhao,Andrew Zhao,Shange Tang,Kaiyu Yang,Aarti Gupta,Zhendong Su,Chi Jin*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: The paper presents a framework for automated code verification in Lean 4, using a hierarchical proof search method powered by an 8-billion parameter model, achieving significant improvements over baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current large language models can generate code but fail to offer correctness guarantees. The gap lies in the challenging task of constructing machine-checkable proofs for code verification, which lacks sufficient automation.

Method: A hierarchical proof search framework decomposes complex verification goals into simpler subgoals. The model, Goedel-Code-Prover-8B, utilizes a principled decomposition score for guiding training and inference. Training involves supervised initialization and hybrid reinforcement learning to stabilize proof generation while promoting exploration.

Result: The model achieves a 62% success rate on three Lean-based code verification benchmarks, a 2.6x improvement over the strongest baseline, and is more efficient than significantly larger neural provers.

Conclusion: This hierarchical framework and its integrated training approach set new benchmarks in automated code verification, demonstrating scalable and efficient performance.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) can generate plausible code but offer limited guarantees of correctness. Formally verifying that implementations satisfy specifications requires constructing machine-checkable proofs, a task that remains beyond current automation. We propose a hierarchical proof search framework for automated code verification in Lean~4 that decomposes complex verification goals into structurally simpler subgoals before attempting tactic-level proving. Central to our approach is a principled decomposition score that combines constructive justification with structural effectiveness. Crucially, this score serves as both the training reward and the inference-time ranking criterion, ensuring strict alignment between optimization and deployment. We train Goedel-Code-Prover-8B, a single unified policy for both decomposition and completion, via supervised initialization followed by hybrid reinforcement learning, where a continuous decomposition reward drives planning exploration while supervised replay stabilizes proof generation. On three Lean-based code verification benchmarks comprising 427 tasks, our 8B-parameter model achieves a 62.0\% prove success rate, a 2.6$\times$ improvement over the strongest baseline, surpassing neural provers up to 84$\times$ larger. We further observe consistent inference-time scaling: success rates improve monotonically with search iterations and sampling budget, with our trained model achieving greater efficiency than frontier off-the-shelf models of comparable scale.

</details>


### [342] [DePro: Understanding the Role of LLMs in Debugging Competitive Programming Code](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.19399)
*Nabiha Parvez,Tanvin Sarkar Pallab,Mia Mohammad Imran,Tarannum Shaila Zaman*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: The paper introduces DePro, a test-case driven debugging approach for competitive programming problems leveraging Large Language Models (LLMs), showing enhanced efficiency and accuracy in error resolution compared to humans and zero-shot LLM debugging.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Understanding the effectiveness of LLMs in debugging tasks, particularly in competitive programming with its diverse challenges and efficiency demands.

Method: The method combines brute-force reference generation, stress testing, and iterative LLM-guided refinement within the DePro framework to debug faulty code submissions.

Result: Experiments reveal DePro consistently reduces debugging attempts by 64% and averages 7.6 minutes saved in debugging time per problem compared to human efforts and zero-shot LLM debugging.

Conclusion: DePro exemplifies how LLMs can enhance debugging efficiency in competitive programming by leveraging test-case driven refinement strategies.

Abstract: Debugging consumes a substantial portion of the software development lifecycle, yet the effectiveness of Large Language Models(LLMs) in this task is not well understood. Competitive programming offers a rich benchmark for such evaluation, given its diverse problem domains and strict efficiency requirements. We present an empirical study of LLM-based debugging on competitive programming problems and introduce DePro, a test-case driven approach that assists programmers by correcting existing code rather than generating new solutions. DePro combines brute-force reference generation, stress testing, and iterative LLM-guided refinement to identify and resolve errors efficiently.Experiments on 13 faulty user submissions from Codeforces demonstrate that DePro consistently produces correct solutions, reducing debugging attempts by up to 64% and debugging time by an average of 7.6 minutes per problem compared to human programmers and zero-shot LLM debugging.

</details>


### [343] [Coordinating Stakeholders in the Consideration of Performance Indicators and Respective Interface Requirements for Automated Vehicles](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.19492)
*Richard Schubert,Marvin Loba,Alexander Blödel,David Klüner,Alexandru Kampmann,Steven Peters*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: The paper introduces a systematic process for coordinating stakeholders on performance indicators for SAE Level 4 autonomous vehicles, focusing on self-awareness and adaptiveness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Enable SAE Level 4 autonomous vehicles to handle external and internal issues via stakeholder coordination on self-perception and performance indicators.

Method: Develop a process-oriented approach with steps, artifacts, and clear documentation for stakeholder communication and traceability, rooted in experience from the autotech.agil project.

Result: Provided lessons learned, identified gaps, and outlined steps for future work based on the applied process.

Conclusion: The proposed systematic process enhances stakeholder communication and decision traceability, contributing to the development of self-aware, adaptive autonomous vehicles.

Abstract: This paper presents a process for coordinating stakeholders in their consideration of performance indicators and respective interface requirements for automated vehicles. These performance indicators are obtained and processed based on the system's self-perception and enable the realization of self-aware and self-adaptive vehicles. This is necessary to allow SAE Level 4 vehicles to handle external disturbances as well as internal degradations and failures at runtime. Without such a systematic process for stakeholder coordination, architectural decisions on realizing self-perception become untraceable and effective communication between stakeholders may be compromised. Our process-oriented approach includes necessary ingredients, steps, and artifacts that explicitly address stakeholder communication, traceability, and knowledge transfer through clear documentation. Our approach is based on the experience gained from applying the process in the autotech.agil project, from which we further present lessons learned, identified gaps, and steps for future work.

</details>


### [344] [Skilled AI Agents for Embedded and IoT Systems Development](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.19583)
*Yiming Li,Yuhan Cheng,Mingchen Ma,Yihang Zou,Ningyuan Yang,Wei Cheng,Hai "Helen" Li,Yiran Chen,Tingjun Chen*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: This paper introduces a skills-based agentic framework and IoT-SkillsBench benchmark to address the challenges faced by AI in hardware-in-the-loop embedded and IoT systems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome the challenges of applying AI-based code generation systems to hardware-in-the-loop embedded and IoT environments, where software must account for specific hardware behaviors, timing constraints, and initialization needs.

Method: Developed a skills-based agentic framework and created IoT-SkillsBench, a benchmark including three embedded platforms, 23 peripherals, and 42 tasks of varying difficulty, with experiments conducted under three agent configurations to evaluate performance via real hardware execution.

Result: Human-expert skills significantly outperformed AI-generated and no-skills configurations, achieving near-perfect success rates across 378 hardware-validated experiments.

Conclusion: Expert-curated, structured skills are essential for achieving reliable software performance in AI systems for embedded and IoT programming, highlighting the limits of current LLM-generated skills for such tasks.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) and agentic systems have shown promise for automated software development, but applying them to hardware-in-the-loop (HIL) embedded and Internet-of-Things (IoT) systems remains challenging due to the tight coupling between software logic and physical hardware behavior. Code that compiles successfully may still fail when deployed on real devices because of timing constraints, peripheral initialization requirements, or hardware-specific behaviors. To address this challenge, we introduce a skills-based agentic framework for HIL embedded development together with IoT-SkillsBench, a benchmark designed to systematically evaluate AI agents in real embedded programming environments. IoT-SkillsBench spans three representative embedded platforms, 23 peripherals, and 42 tasks across three difficulty levels, where each task is evaluated under three agent configurations (no-skills, LLM-generated skills, and human-expert skills) and validated through real hardware execution. Across 378 hardware validated experiments, we show that concise human-expert skills with structured expert knowledge enable near-perfect success rates across platforms.

</details>


### [345] [GazePrinter: Visualizing Expert Gaze to Guide Novices in a New Codebase](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.19855)
*Peng Kuang,Emma Söderberg,April Yi Wang,Martin Höst*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: The paper proposes GazePrinter, a tool leveraging expert gaze patterns to help novice programmers understand new codebases, showing benefits like reduced cognitive load and improved code traversal.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To aid program comprehension for novice programmers by exploring gaze-based tools inspired by expert visual attention patterns.

Method: Develop and study GazePrinter, which visualizes expert gaze cues for novices, using mixed-methods: surveys, controlled experiments, and interviews with 40 participants.

Result: GazePrinter influenced novice programmers to follow paths similar to experts, reducing time spent and cognitive load during tasks.

Conclusion: Visualizing expert gaze enhances program comprehension in novices, suggesting potential in gaze-based collaborative tools.

Abstract: Program comprehension is an essential activity in software engineering. Not only does it often challenge professionals, but it can also hinder novices from advancing their programming skills. Gaze, an emerging modality in developer tools, has so far primarily been utilized to improve our understanding of programmers' visual attention and as a means to reason about programmers' cognitive processes. There has been limited exploration of integrating gaze-based assistance into development environments to support programmers, despite the tight links between attention and gaze. We also know that joint attention is important in collaboration, further suggesting that there is value in exploring collective gaze.
  In this paper, we investigate the effect of visualizing gaze patterns gathered from experts to novice programmers to assist them with program comprehension in a new codebase. To this end, we present GazePrinter, designed to provide gaze-orienting visual cues informed by experts to aid novices with program comprehension. We present the results of a mixed-methods study conducted with 40 novices to study the effects of using GazePrinter for program comprehension tasks. The study included a survey, a controlled experiment, and interviews. We found that visualization of expert gaze can have a significant effect on novice programmers' behavior in terms of which path they take through the code base; with GazePrinter, novices took a path closer to the path taken by experts. We also found indications of reduced time and cognitive load among novices using GazePrinter.

</details>


### [346] [Revealing Domain-Spatiality Patterns for Configuration Tuning: Domain Knowledge Meets Fitness Landscapes](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.19897)
*Yulong Ye,Hongyuan Liang,Chao Jiang,Miqing Li,Tao Chen*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: Domland leverages Fitness Landscape Analysis alongside domain-driven methodologies to understand configuration tuning challenges, offering insights into tuner effectiveness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To unravel tuners' effectiveness in quality assurance by addressing the black-box nature of configurable systems using comprehensible and generalizable methods.

Method: Employs Fitness Landscape Analysis integrated with domain-driven insights in a two-pronged approach to characterize configuration tuning cases.

Result: Key findings include the system-specific nature of configuration landscapes, core options influencing landscape ruggedness, and variable workload effects depending on system.

Conclusion: Domland improves interpretation of configuration challenges and informs tuner design, offering systematic insights into system-specific tuning behaviors.

Abstract: Configuration tuning for better performance is crucial in quality assurance. Yet, there has long been a mystery on tuners' effectiveness, due to the black-box nature of configurable systems. Prior efforts predominantly adopt static domain analysis (e.g., static taint analysis), which often lacks generalizability, or dynamic data analysis (e.g., benchmarking performance analysis), limiting explainability. In this work, we embrace Fitness Landscape Analysis (FLA) as a bridge between domain knowledge and difficulty of the tuning. We propose Domland, a two-pronged methodology that synergizes the spatial information obtained from FLA and domain-driven analysis to systematically capture the hidden characteristics of configuration tuning cases, explaining how and why a tuner might succeed or fail. This helps to better interpret and contextualize the behavior of tuners and inform tuner design. To evaluate Domland, we conduct a case study of nine software systems and 93 workloads, from which we reveal several key findings: (1) configuration landscapes are inherently system-specific, with no single domain factor (e.g., system area, programming language, or resource intensity) consistently shaping their structure; (2) the core options (e.g., pic-struct of x264), which control the main functional flows, exert a stronger influence on landscape ruggedness (i.e. the difficulty of tuning) compared to resource options (e.g., cpu-independent of x264); (3) Workload effects on landscape structure are not uniformly tied to type or scale. Both contribute to landscape variations, but their impact is system-dependent.

</details>


### [347] [Teaching Practically Relevant Research Problem Formulation in Software Engineering with Lean Research Inception](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.19967)
*Anrafel Fernandes Pereira,Tatiane Ornelas,Allysson Allex Araujo,Marcos Kalinowski*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: Research applied Lean Research Inception (LRI) to teach Software Engineering (SE) students formulate industry-relevant research problems, reporting positive results in clarity and alignment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the gap between industry and academia by helping SE students formulate well-aligned and relevant research problems.

Method: A case study involving 60 students and 7 faculty advisors at a Brazilian university to test the use of Lean Research Inception (LRI).

Result: Students experienced improvements in reasoning (60%), clarity and definition (61.7%), contextualization (60%), and communication (50%). Faculty advisors observed more structured problem statements (57.1%) and provided strong recommendations (85.7%).

Conclusion: LRI shows potential for significantly supporting the creation of practice-aligned research problems, enhancing SE education and bridging the industry-academia gap.

Abstract: [Background] Well-formulated Software Engineering (SE) research problems are essential for bridging the gap between industry-academia. Lean Research Inception (LRI) aims to support this activity. [Goal] Apply LRI to support SE students in formulating practice-aligned research problems. [Method] We conducted a case study with 60 students and 7 faculty advisors of a Brazilian university. [Results] Students reported benefits in reasoning (60%), clarity and definition (61.7%), contextualization (60%), and communication (50%). Advisors also observed clearer and more structured problems (57.1%) with a high recommendation rate (85.7%). [Conclusion] LRI can be a promising approach to support practice-aligned research problem formulation in SE education.

</details>


### [348] [Orchestrating Human-AI Software Delivery: A Retrospective Longitudinal Field Study of Three Software Modernization Programs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.20028)
*Maximiliano Armesto,Christophe Kolb*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: The study investigated the impact of an AI platform, Chiron, used collaboratively with humans in software delivery across four stages, showing its effectiveness in improving project speed, quality, and effort reduction.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional AI in software engineering is task-specific, and there is limited evidence on its impact on team-level delivery. The paper aims to study the longitudinal, practical benefits of combining AI and human collaboration across the full software delivery workflow.

Method: The research is based on a longitudinal field study of an AI platform, Chiron, compared across traditional and four evolving platform configurations (V1-V4) during real software modernization programs. The study employed both observed and modeled outcomes to analyze impacts.

Result: The results highlight significant improvements with AI-human collaboration through Chiron: reduced project duration (36.0 to 9.3 weeks), decreased raw and senior-equivalent effort (1080.0 to 232.5 and to 139.5 SEE-days, respectively), reduced issues (8.03 to 2.09 per 100 tasks), and improved initial release coverage (77.0% to 90.5%).

Conclusion: The findings suggest that integrating AI into orchestrated workflows yields greater benefits compared to deploying it as isolated tools. The Chiron platform demonstrates consistent gains in speed, coverage, and issue reduction through its iterative improvements.

Abstract: Evidence on AI in software engineering still leans heavily toward individual task completion, while evidence on team-level delivery remains scarce. We report a retrospective longitudinal field study of Chiron, an industrial platform that coordinates humans and AI agents across four delivery stages: analysis, planning, implementation, and validation. The study covers three real software modernization programs -- a COBOL banking migration (~30k LOC), a large accounting modernization (~400k LOC), and a .NET/Angular mortgage modernization (~30k LOC) -- observed across five delivery configurations: a traditional baseline and four successive platform versions (V1--V4). The benchmark separates observed outcomes (stage durations, task volumes, validation-stage issues, first-release coverage) from modeled outcomes (person-days and senior-equivalent effort under explicit staffing scenarios). Under baseline staffing assumptions, portfolio totals move from 36.0 to 9.3 summed project-weeks; modeled raw effort falls from 1080.0 to 232.5 person-days; modeled senior-equivalent effort falls from 1080.0 to 139.5 SEE-days; validation-stage issue load falls from 8.03 to 2.09 issues per 100 tasks; and first-release coverage rises from 77.0% to 90.5%. V3 and V4 add acceptance-criteria validation, repository-native review, and hybrid human-agent execution, simultaneously improving speed, coverage, and issue load. The evidence supports a central thesis: the largest gains appear when AI is embedded in an orchestrated workflow rather than deployed as an isolated coding assistant.

</details>


### [349] [Agentic Harness for Real-World Compilers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.20075)
*Yingwei Zheng,Cong Li,Shaohua Li,Yuqun Zhang,Zhendong Su*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: The paper introduces llvm-autofix, a tool designed to help LLMs fix compiler bugs in LLVM, achieving better performance compared to existing methods while highlighting challenges unique to compiler bug repair.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Compiler bugs are challenging to fix due to their complexity and sparse bug reports, and existing general LLM approaches fail to perform adequately in this domain.

Method: The authors created llvm-autofix, comprising LLVM-specific tools, a reproducible bug benchmark, and a minimal agent optimized for debugging compiler-specific issues.

Result: Evaluations reveal LLMs perform 60% worse on compiler bugs than general software bugs; llvm-autofix-mini achieves a 22% performance improvement over state-of-the-art methods.

Conclusion: Specialized frameworks like llvm-autofix are essential to enhance LLM performance in specialized domains like compiler engineering, setting a foundation for further advancement in complex systems.

Abstract: Compilers are critical to modern computing, yet fixing compiler bugs is difficult. While recent large language model (LLM) advancements enable automated bug repair, compiler bugs pose unique challenges due to their complexity, deep cross-domain expertise requirements, and sparse, non-descriptive bug reports, necessitating compiler-specific tools. To bridge the gap, we introduce llvm-autofix, the first agentic harness designed to assist LLM agents in understanding and fixing compiler bugs. Our focus is on LLVM, one of the most widely used compiler infrastructures. Central to llvm-autofix are agent-friendly LLVM tools, a benchmark llvm-bench of reproducible LLVM bugs, and a tailored minimal agent llvm-autofix-mini for fixing LLVM bugs. Our evaluation demonstrates a performance decline of 60% in frontier models when tackling compiler bugs compared with common software bugs. Our minimal agent llvm-autofix-mini also outperforms the state-of-the-art by approximately 22%. This emphasizes the necessity for specialized harnesses like ours to close the loop between LLMs and compiler engineering. We believe this work establishes a foundation for advancing LLM capabilities in complex systems like compilers. GitHub: https://github.com/dtcxzyw/llvm-autofix

</details>


<div id='q-bio.NC'></div>

# q-bio.NC [[Back]](#toc)

### [350] [Analytically tractable model of synaptic crowding explains emergent small-world structure and network dynamics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.19320)
*Makoto Fukushima*

Main category: q-bio.NC

TL;DR: The paper presents a one-parameter wiring rule for neural circuits, accounting for synaptic crowding. It explains how connectivity, distance, and dynamics emerge in networks under such constraints.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address how neural networks balance local connectivity and global integration while considering synaptic crowding and its developmental constraints.

Method: Introduces a single-parameter model to explain synaptic crowding effects, derives scaling laws, explores spatial proximity during connectivity, and connects the model to network dynamics.

Result: The model predicts logarithmic growth in mean connectivity, bounded variance, power-law distribution in connection lengths, small-world network traits, and links network structure to dynamic properties.

Conclusion: The study provides a theoretical framework that connects synaptic crowding constraints to large-scale neural network organization and dynamics, with testable predictions for neuroscience.

Abstract: Neural circuits must balance local connectivity constraints against the need for global integration. Here we introduce a minimal wiring rule motivated by synaptic crowding: as a neuron accumulates incoming connections, each additional synapse becomes progressively harder to form. This single-parameter model admits an exact finite-size solution for the induced in-degree distribution and yields simple scaling laws: mean connectivity grows only logarithmically with network size while variance remains bounded -- consistent with homeostatic regulation of synaptic density. When candidates are encountered in order of spatial proximity, the crowding rule produces a broad, approximately power-law distribution of connection lengths without prescribing any explicit distance-dependent wiring law; combined with shortcut rewiring, this yields networks with small-world characteristics. We further show that the induced degree statistics largely determine attractor basin boundaries in threshold network dynamics, while local clustering primarily modulates the prevalence of long-lived non-absorbing outcomes near these boundaries. The model provides testable predictions linking local developmental constraints to macroscopic network organization and dynamics.

</details>


### [351] [Curvature Sensitive Cells in the Modular Structures of The Visual Cortex](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.19425)
*Giovanna Citti,Vasiliki Liontou*

Main category: q-bio.NC

TL;DR: The paper models curvature-sensitive cells in the primary visual cortex using advanced mathematical structures to explain their functional architecture, connectivity, and receptive profiles.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To provide a geometric and mathematical explanation for the organization and functioning of curvature-sensitive cells in the visual cortex.

Method: The research constructs a geometric model using the cotangent bundle associated with oriented contact elements in the retina, identifying an Engel structure and utilizing the Lie algebra of SIM(2) for analysis.

Result: It establishes SIM(2) as the symmetry group for curvature-sensitive cells and derives their receptive profiles through an uncertainty principle adapted to this symmetry.

Conclusion: The paper advances our understanding of curvature-sensitive cells by linking their functional architecture to geometric structures and symmetry principles.

Abstract: We propose a model of the functional architecture of curvature-sensitive cells in the primary visual cortex. The model accounts for the modular and hierarchical organization of the cortex, the horizontal connectivity, and the shape of receptive profiles of these cells as Gabor-type filters. We construct a canonical affine subbundle of the cotangent bundle of the manifold of oriented contact elements of the retina as a geometric model for these cells, and show that this subbundle carries an Engel structure related to that of the Cartan prolongation. On an open submanifold of the Cartan prolongation, we identify generators of the Engel distribution whose iterated Lie brackets span the Lie algebra of SIM(2). The identification of sim(2) as the Lie algebra of these generators determines SIM(2) as the natural symmetry group for curvature-sensitive cells. Finally, we characterize the receptive profiles of curvature-sensitive cells as minima of a SIM(2)-adapted uncertainty principle applied to the generators of the Engel structure.

</details>


### [352] [A Unified Phase-native Computational Principle Governs Hippocampal Spike Timing and Neural Coding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.19690)
*Reza Ahmadvand,Sara Safura Sharif,Yaser Mike Banad*

Main category: q-bio.NC

TL;DR: The paper develops a unified framework that explains how hippocampal neurons encode information through spike timing and magnitude using forced phase integration.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand the computational principle behind the precise phase-locking of hippocampal neurons to network oscillations and address limitations of prior models.

Method: The authors introduce a biologically grounded generative framework called the unified complex-valued neuron (UCN) to separate neural information into magnitude (what) and phase (when) dimensions.

Result: The UCN framework reproduces biological spike-theta synchronization and challenges previous slope-locking association explanations, asserting these effects arise from oscillatory contamination.

Conclusion: A unified principle for neural timing and coding is established, re-evaluating previous findings to provide clarity on how neural information is encoded.

Abstract: Hippocampal neurons exhibit precise phase locking to network oscillations, but the computational principle governing this temporal precision is still unclear. Neural information is conveyed jointly by firing rates and spike timing, but existing models treat these dimensions separately, limiting mechanistic interpretation of spike-field coupling and its reported association with spectral features such as the aperiodic slope. Here we show that hippocampal phase locking emerges from a fundamental dynamical mechanism referred to as forced phase integration that separates neural information into orthogonal magnitude (what) and phase (when) coordinates. To formalize this principle, the unified complex-valued neuron (UCN) has been developed, a biologically grounded generative framework in which spike timing arises from phase accumulation while spike magnitude encodes instantaneous signal strength. This framework reproduces biological spike-theta synchronization and enables mechanistic re-evaluation of slope-locking associations, demonstrating that previously reported effects arise from oscillatory contamination rather than causal modulation. These findings establish a unified phase-native principle of neural timing and coding.

</details>


### [353] [Problem difficulty and waiting time shape the level of detail and temporal organization of visual strategies in human planning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.19881)
*Mattia Eluchans,Giovanni Pezzulo*

Main category: q-bio.NC

TL;DR: The study examines how problem difficulty and time constraints impact visual strategies for planning in a grid-based problem-solving task, revealing differences in planning and execution performance based on these factors.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand how problem constraints, like difficulty and time, influence visual strategies during planning, particularly regarding plan coverage and temporal organization.

Method: Participants performed a grid task while their cursor and eye movements were tracked. Problem difficulty was manipulated using a novel construct called misleadingness, and time constraints were varied by allowing immediate or delayed action.

Result: Higher difficulty reduced success rates and increased plan corrections, pauses, and pre-movement inspections. Time constraints impacted replanning and the alignment of gaze with execution, with delayed actions allowing more efficient pre-movement planning.

Conclusion: Problem difficulty promotes broader visual plan coverage, while time availability modulates the extent of planning and replanning during task execution, influencing gaze-path coherence and problem-solving strategies.

Abstract: Planning entails identifying sequences of actions to reach a goal, yet we still have incomplete knowledge of how problem constraints, such as difficulty and available time, influence the visual strategies supporting plan construction, both in terms of coverage of the to-be-executed plans and its temporal organization. To fill this gap, we recorded participants' cursor and eye movements in a multi-target problem solving task on a grid. We manipulated two orthogonal dimensions: problem difficulty, by introducing the novel construct of misleadingness, which measures how nodes' distances on the grid diverged from their relative position along the solution, and waiting time, by allowing participants either to act immediately or wait before moving. We found that difficulty significantly affected both performance and gaze: harder problems reduced success rates, required more corrections and pauses, elicited longer pre-movement inspection that provided higher coverage of the to-be-executed plan, and more re-fixations. When participants could start immediately, they did so without fully consolidating their plan. This led to more pauses and backtracks, but also to more precise gaze-cursor alignment during execution, suggesting improved online control compensating for incomplete planning. With increased planning time, greater difficulty led participants to achieve a better temporal alignment between pre-movement visual inspection and cursor movement during execution. Overall, our results suggest that problem difficulty increases the visual coverage of the upcoming plan, whereas time availability shapes the extent of replanning during execution and determines whether gaze-path coherence emerges before movement or only during execution in difficult problems.

</details>


<div id='stat.ML'></div>

# stat.ML [[Back]](#toc)

### [354] [Pseudo-Labeling for Unsupervised Domain Adaptation with Kernel GLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.19422)
*Nathan Weill,Kaizheng Wang*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: The paper introduces a framework for unsupervised domain adaptation in kernel Generalized Linear Models (GLMs) under covariate shift, leveraging labeled source data, unlabeled target data, pseudo-labels, and excess-risk bounds.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address prediction error minimization in domain adaptation scenarios where data from the source and target domains differ in covariate distributions.

Method: The framework leverages labeled source data split into two batches: one for training candidate models and one for building an imputation model, which generates pseudo-labels for target data to enable adaptive model selection.

Result: Experiments on synthetic and real datasets show the framework improves prediction accuracy compared to source-only models, while non-asymptotic excess-risk bounds provide theoretical performance guarantees.

Conclusion: The framework provides a principled and effective approach to tackle unsupervised domain adaptation under covariate shift in kernel GLMs, yielding consistent performance improvements and theoretical validation.

Abstract: We propose a principled framework for unsupervised domain adaptation under covariate shift in kernel Generalized Linear Models (GLMs), encompassing kernelized linear, logistic, and Poisson regression with ridge regularization. Our goal is to minimize prediction error in the target domain by leveraging labeled source data and unlabeled target data, despite differences in covariate distributions. We partition the labeled source data into two batches: one for training a family of candidate models, and the other for building an imputation model. This imputation model generates pseudo-labels for the target data, enabling robust model selection. We establish non-asymptotic excess-risk bounds that characterize adaptation performance through an "effective labeled sample size", explicitly accounting for the unknown covariate shift. Experiments on synthetic and real datasets demonstrate consistent performance gains over source-only baselines.

</details>


### [355] [Subspace Projection Methods for Fast Spectral Embeddings of Evolving Graphs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.19439)
*Mohammad Eini,Abdullah Karaaslanli,Vassilis Kalantzis,Panagiotis A. Traganitis*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: This paper proposes a novel algorithm for dynamically updating the eigenvectors of evolving graph matrices, ensuring lower computational and memory costs with robust downstream-task accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Dynamic graphs often necessitate frequent updates to eigenvector-related calculations, which classical eigendecomposition methods cannot efficiently handle.

Method: The method employs Rayleigh-Ritz projections by projecting the original eigenvalue problem onto a subspace that captures the invariant subspace related to the desired eigenvectors, leveraging eigenvector perturbation analysis.

Result: The algorithm demonstrates lower computational and memory complexity than alternatives, with strong performance in eigenvector approximation and downstream tasks like central node identification and node clustering.

Conclusion: The proposed framework provides an effective and computationally efficient approach for handling eigenvector updates in evolving graph scenarios.

Abstract: Several graph data mining, signal processing, and machine learning downstream tasks rely on information related to the eigenvectors of the associated adjacency or Laplacian matrix. Classical eigendecomposition methods are powerful when the matrix remains static but cannot be applied to problems where the matrix entries are updated or the number of rows and columns increases frequently. Such scenarios occur routinely in graph analytics when the graph is changing dynamically and either edges and/or nodes are being added and removed. This paper puts forth a new algorithmic framework to update the eigenvectors associated with the leading eigenvalues of an initial adjacency or Laplacian matrix as the graph evolves dynamically. The proposed algorithm is based on Rayleigh-Ritz projections, in which the original eigenvalue problem is projected onto a restricted subspace which ideally encapsulates the invariant subspace associated with the sought eigenvectors. Following ideas from eigenvector perturbation analysis, we present a new methodology to build the projection subspace. The proposed framework features lower computational and memory complexity with respect to competitive alternatives while empirical results show strong qualitative performance, both in terms of eigenvector approximation and accuracy of downstream learning tasks of central node identification and node clustering.

</details>


### [356] [Near-Equivalent Q-learning Policies for Dynamic Treatment Regimes](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.19440)
*Sophia Yazzourh,Erica E. M. Moodie*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: The paper introduces an extension to the Q-learning framework for precision medicine, allowing identification of multiple near-optimal treatment strategies instead of a single optimal one.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current Q-learning methods in precision medicine focus on deriving a single optimal treatment sequence, which may hide clinically meaningful alternatives when multiple options yield similar outcomes.

Method: The authors propose a worst-value tolerance criterion, controlled by a hyperparameter ε, which allows for identifying sets of ε-optimal policies within a defined acceptable performance range. The Q-learning process is adapted to a matrix-valued representation for this purpose.

Result: The framework creates families of near-equivalent treatment strategies and explicitly highlights regions where multiple decisions result in similar outcomes. This was demonstrated in a single-stage decision problem and a simulated multi-stage oncology model involving tumor size and treatment toxicity.

Conclusion: The approach effectively broadens the scope of Q-learning in precision medicine by accommodating multiple treatment strategies, thereby increasing flexibility and acknowledging clinical decision-making complexities.

Abstract: Precision medicine aims to tailor therapeutic decisions to individual patient characteristics. This objective is commonly formalized through dynamic treatment regimes, which use statistical and machine learning methods to derive sequential decision rules adapted to evolving clinical information. In most existing formulations, these approaches produce a single optimal treatment at each stage, leading to a unique decision sequence. However, in many clinical settings, several treatment options may yield similar expected outcomes, and focusing on a single optimal policy may conceal meaningful alternatives. We extend the Q-learning framework for retrospective data by introducing a worst-value tolerance criterion controlled by a hyperparameter $\varepsilon$, which specifies the maximum acceptable deviation from the optimal expected value. Rather than identifying a single optimal policy, the proposed approach constructs sets of $\varepsilon$-optimal policies whose performance remains within a controlled neighborhood of the optimum. This formulation shifts Q-learning from a vector-valued representation to a matrix-valued one, allowing multiple admissible value functions to coexist during backward recursion. The approach yields families of near-equivalent treatment strategies and explicitly identifies regions of treatment indifference where several decisions achieve comparable outcomes. We illustrate the framework in two settings: a single-stage problem highlighting indifference regions around the decision boundary, and a multi-stage decision process based on a simulated oncology model describing tumor size and treatment toxicity dynamics.

</details>


### [357] [On the role of memorization in learned priors for geophysical inverse problems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.19629)
*Ali Siahkoohi,Davide Sabeddu*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: The paper investigates the challenges of using deep generative models for seismic inversion, showing that memorization of training examples leads to biased posterior distributions, with specific implications for diffusion models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the scarcity of representative subsurface model datasets in geoscience and the potential for deep generative models to memorize such limited data rather than capturing geological distributions.

Method: Analyzed the memorization behavior in generative models, specifically diffusion models, and derived closed-form mathematical representations of priors and posteriors under memorization.

Result: Demonstrated that memorized generative priors reduce to likelihood-weighted reweighted distributions, and validated findings through experiments on inverse problems and full waveform inversion.

Conclusion: Memorization in generative models leads to biased geological posterior distributions, posing challenges for seismic inversion applications.

Abstract: Learned priors based on deep generative models offer data-driven regularization for seismic inversion, but training them requires a dataset of representative subsurface models -- a resource that is inherently scarce in geoscience applications. Since the training objective of most generative models can be cast as maximum likelihood on a finite dataset, any such model risks converging to the empirical distribution -- effectively memorizing the training examples rather than learning the underlying geological distribution. We show that the posterior under such a memorized prior reduces to a reweighted empirical distribution -- i.e., a likelihood-weighted lookup among the stored training examples. For diffusion models specifically, memorization yields a Gaussian mixture prior in closed form, and linearizing the forward operator around each training example gives a Gaussian mixture posterior whose components have widths and shifts governed by the local Jacobian. We validate these predictions on a stylized inverse problem and demonstrate the consequences of memorization through diffusion posterior sampling for full waveform inversion.

</details>


### [358] [Model Selection and Parameter Estimation of Multi-dimensional Gaussian Mixture Model](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.19657)
*Xinyu Liu,Hai Zhang*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: The paper addresses the learning of multi-dimensional Gaussian Mixture Models (GMMs), providing a sample-efficient algorithm with optimal performance guarantees.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To improve model order selection and parameter estimation in Gaussian Mixture Models (GMMs), especially in high-dimensional settings, while achieving computational efficiency and theoretical optimality.

Method: Introduced a thresholding-based spectral gap estimator for model order selection and a gradient-based optimization for parameter estimation, supplemented with PCA for high-dimensional settings.

Result: The proposed method achieves minimax optimal sample complexity and parametric convergence rates, outperforming Expectation-Maximization (EM) in accuracy and computational efficiency.

Conclusion: The paper offers a theoretically and practically efficient framework for learning GMMs, optimizing both model order selection and parameter estimation, particularly in high-dimensional scenarios.

Abstract: In this paper, we study the problem of learning multi-dimensional Gaussian Mixture Models (GMMs), with a specific focus on model order selection and efficient mixing distribution estimation. We first establish an information-theoretic lower bound on the critical sample complexity required for reliable model selection. More specifically, we show that distinguishing a $k$-component mixture from a simpler model necessitates a sample size scaling of $Ω(Δ^{-(4k-4)})$. We then propose a thresholding-based estimation algorithm that evaluates the spectral gap of an empirical covariance matrix constructed from random Fourier measurement vectors. This parameter-free estimator operates with an efficient time complexity of $\mathcal{O}(k^2 n)$, scaling linearly with the sample size. We demonstrate that the sample complexity of our method matches the established lower bound, confirming its minimax optimality with respect to the component separation distance $Δ$.
  Conditioned on the estimated model order, we subsequently introduce a gradient-based minimization method for parameter estimation. To effectively navigate the non-convex objective landscape, we employ a data-driven, score-based initialization strategy that guarantees rapid convergence. We prove that this method achieves the optimal parametric convergence rate of $\mathcal{O}_p(n^{-1/2})$ for estimating the component means. To enhance the algorithm's efficiency in high-dimensional regimes where the ambient dimension exceeds the number of mixture components (i.e., \(d > k\)), we integrate principal component analysis (PCA) for dimension reduction.
