Numerical experiments demonstrate that our Fourier-based algorithmic framework outperforms conventional Expectation-Maximization (EM) methods in both estimation accuracy and computational time.

</details>


### [359] [A two-step sequential approach for hyperparameter selection in finite context models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.19736)
*José Contente,Ana Martins,Armando J. Pinho,Sónia Gouveia*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: The paper introduces a computationally efficient two-step method for hyperparameter selection in Finite-context models (FCMs), achieving comparable compression performance with reduced computational cost.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Hyperparameter selection in Finite-context models is critical but computationally expensive, especially with increasing model complexity.

Method: A sequential two-step approach: (1) Estimate context length using dependence measures, and (2) select smoothing parameter via maximum likelihood, conditional on the chosen context length.

Result: Simulation experiments demonstrated improved hyperparameter estimation sensitivity with increased sample sizes and compression on par with exhaustive grid searches, but with far lower computational demands.

Conclusion: The proposed sequential method provides an effective and computationally efficient alternative for hyperparameter tuning in FCMs.

Abstract: Finite-context models (FCMs) are widely used for compressing symbolic sequences such as DNA, where predictive performance depends critically on the context length k and smoothing parameter α. In practice, these hyperparameters are typically selected through exhaustive search, which is computationally expensive and scales poorly with model complexity. This paper proposes a statistically grounded two-step sequential approach for efficient hyperparameter selection in FCMs. The key idea is to decompose the joint optimization problem into two independent stages. First, the context length k is estimated using categorical serial dependence measures, including Cramér's ν, Cohen's \k{appa} and partial mutual information (pami). Second, the smoothing parameter α is estimated via maximum likelihood conditional on the selected context length k. Simulation experiments were conducted on synthetic symbolic sequences generated by FCMs across multiple (k, α) configurations, considering a four-letter alphabet and different sample sizes. Results show that the dependence measures are substantially more sensitive to variations in k than in α, supporting the sequential estimation strategy. As expected, the accuracy of the hyperparameter estimation improves with increasing sample size. Furthermore, the proposed method achieves compression performance comparable to exhaustive grid search in terms of average bitrate (bits per symbol), while substantially reducing computational cost. Overall, the results on simulated data show that the proposed sequential approach is a practical and computationally efficient alternative to exhaustive hyperparameter tuning in FCMs.

</details>


### [360] [Explainable cluster analysis: a bagging approach](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.19840)
*Federico Maria Quetti,Elena Ballante,Silvia Figini,Paolo Giudici*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: The paper introduces an ensemble-based clustering method to address the lack of explainability in traditional clustering by integrating bagging and feature dropout to provide feature importance scores.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to overcome the limitation of clustering approaches that fail to provide insights into the features driving similarity groupings.

Method: The method combines bootstrap resampling techniques and feature dropout with an information-theoretic approach, using mutual information weighted by clustering validity, to assess feature importance and generate consensus partitions.

Result: The method improves stability and robustness in cluster definitions, especially for small-sample or noisy datasets, and offers feature importance scores alongside the clustering output.

Conclusion: The proposed framework successfully combines clustering structure with feature relevance interpretation, validated across simulated and real-world datasets.

Abstract: A major limitation of clustering approaches is their lack of explainability: methods rarely provide insight into which features drive the grouping of similar observations. To address this limitation, we propose an ensemble-based clustering framework that integrates bagging and feature dropout to generate feature importance scores, in analogy with feature importance mechanisms in supervised random forests. By leveraging multiple bootstrap resampling schemes and aggregating the resulting partitions, the method improves stability and robustness of the cluster definition, particularly in small-sample or noisy settings. Feature importance is assessed through an information-theoretic approach: at each step, the mutual information between each feature and the estimated cluster labels is computed and weighted by a measure of clustering validity to emphasize well-formed partitions, before being aggregated into a final score. The method outputs both a consensus partition and a corresponding measure of feature importance, enabling a unified interpretation of clustering structure and variable relevance. Its effectiveness is demonstrated on multiple simulated and real-world datasets.

</details>


### [361] [Minimax Generalized Cross-Entropy](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.19874)
*Kartheek Bondugula,Santiago Mazuelas,Aritz Pérez,Anqi Liu*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: The paper introduces a minimax generalized cross-entropy (MGCE) loss function designed to address issues in optimization and robustness observed in prior formulations like CE, MAE, and GCE, achieving better performance on complex datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing loss functions like CE and MAE have optimization and robustness challenges, with GCE trading off between these aspects but suffering underfitting in complex datasets.

Method: The authors propose MGCE, a minimax formulation of GCE, resulting in a convex optimization solved efficiently using stochastic gradient via implicit differentiation.

Result: Experiments on benchmark datasets demonstrate that MGCE achieves high accuracy, faster convergence, and improved calibration, particularly under label noise conditions.

Conclusion: MGCE effectively balances robustness and optimization challenges, presenting a reliable alternative for complex and noisy classification tasks.

Abstract: Loss functions play a central role in supervised classification. Cross-entropy (CE) is widely used, whereas the mean absolute error (MAE) loss can offer robustness but is difficult to optimize. Interpolating between the CE and MAE losses, generalized cross-entropy (GCE) has recently been introduced to provide a trade-off between optimization difficulty and robustness. Existing formulations of GCE result in a non-convex optimization over classification margins that is prone to underfitting, leading to poor performances with complex datasets. In this paper, we propose a minimax formulation of generalized cross-entropy (MGCE) that results in a convex optimization over classification margins. Moreover, we show that MGCEs can provide an upper bound on the classification error. The proposed bilevel convex optimization can be efficiently implemented using stochastic gradient computed via implicit differentiation. Using benchmark datasets, we show that MGCE achieves strong accuracy, faster convergence, and better calibration, especially in the presence of label noise.

</details>


### [362] [Deep Autocorrelation Modeling for Time-Series Forecasting: Progress and Prospects](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.19899)
*Hao Wang,Licheng Pan,Qingsong Wen,Jialin Yu,Zhichao Chen,Chunyuan Zheng,Xiaoxi Li,Zhixuan Chu,Chao Xu,Mingming Gong,Haoxuan Li,Yuan Lu,Zhouchen Lin,Philip Torr,Yan Liu*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: The paper reviews recent advancements in deep time-series forecasting focusing on autocorrelation modeling in both input data and labels, addressing the challenges of neural architecture design and learning objectives.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Autocorrelation in time-series data poses modeling challenges that require systematic assessment, especially regarding the design of neural architectures and learning objectives for deeper insights.

Method: The authors create a novel taxonomy to classify research on both model architectures and learning objectives, and provide a unified, autocorrelation-centered review of the evolution of deep time-series forecasting.

Result: The paper identifies gaps in existing literature, offers a novel taxonomy, and systematically analyzes motivations and trends in autocorrelation modeling research.

Conclusion: This study provides a comprehensive resource for understanding the field of autocorrelation modeling in time-series forecasting and highlights the evolution and future trends in this domain.

Abstract: Autocorrelation is a defining characteristic of time-series data, where each observation is statistically dependent on its predecessors. In the context of deep time-series forecasting, autocorrelation arises in both the input history and the label sequences, presenting two central research challenges: (1) designing neural architectures that model autocorrelation in history sequences, and (2) devising learning objectives that model autocorrelation in label sequences. Recent studies have made strides in tackling these challenges, but a systematic survey examining both aspects remains lacking. To bridge this gap, this paper provides a comprehensive review of deep time-series forecasting from the perspective of autocorrelation modeling. In contrast to existing surveys, this work makes two distinctive contributions. First, it proposes a novel taxonomy that encompasses recent literature on both model architectures and learning objectives -- whereas prior surveys neglect or inadequately discuss the latter aspect. Second, it offers a thorough analysis of the motivations, insights, and progression of the surveyed literature from a unified, autocorrelation-centric perspective, providing a holistic overview of the evolution of deep time-series forecasting. The full list of papers and resources is available at https://github.com/Master-PLC/Awesome-TSF-Papers.

</details>


### [363] [Graph-Informed Adversarial Modeling: Infimal Subadditivity of Interpolative Divergences](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.20025)
*Panagiota Birmpa,Eric Joseph Hall*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: The paper explores adversarial learning aligned with Bayesian network structures by introducing a family-level discrepancy that can replace global discrepancy in specific conditions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to address the inefficiencies in current adversarial methods, such as GANs, by leveraging the structure of known Bayesian networks.

Method: The study proves an infimal subadditivity principle and presents a graph-informed design for GANs, replacing monolithic discriminators with localized family-level discriminators for more accurate alignment with Bayesian networks.

Result: Theoretical results confirm the surrogate’s accuracy and experiments demonstrate improved stability and structural recovery in graph-informed GANs relative to graph-agnostic counterparts.

Conclusion: Graph-informed GANs, guided by Bayesian network structures and family-level discrepancies, offer a more stable and effective approach compared to traditional methods.

Abstract: We study adversarial learning when the target distribution factorizes according to a known Bayesian network. For interpolative divergences, including $(f,Γ)$-divergences, we prove a new infimal subadditivity principle showing that, under suitable conditions, a global variational discrepancy is controlled by an average of family-level discrepancies aligned with the graph. In an additive regime, this surrogate is exact. This provides a variational justification for replacing a graph-agnostic GAN with a monolithic discriminator by a graph-informed GAN with localized family-level discriminators. The result does not require the optimizer itself to factorize according to the graph. We also obtain parallel results for integral probability metrics and proximal optimal transport divergences, identify natural discriminator classes for which the theory applies, and present experiments showing improved stability and structural recovery relative to graph-agnostic baselines.

</details>


<div id='q-bio.QM'></div>

# q-bio.QM [[Back]](#toc)

### [364] [Grounded Multimodal Retrieval-Augmented Drafting of Radiology Impressions Using Case-Based Similarity Search](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.17765)
*Himadri Samanta*

Main category: q-bio.QM

TL;DR: The paper introduces a retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) system to improve reliability and factual grounding in automated radiology report generation for chest radiographs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Fully generative automated radiology report systems often produce hallucinations and lack clinical grounding, which undermines their reliability for real-world application.

Method: The proposed system leverages multimodal retrieval combining image-text embeddings (using CLIP), similarity retrieval, and citation-constrained draft generation. A curated subset of the MIMIC-CXR dataset was used, employing FAISS indexing for scalable retrieval and ensuring citation traceability and safety mechanisms in output.

Result: The multimodal retrieval system exhibited high retrieval performance with Recall@5 above 0.95 and produced interpretable, trustworthy draft impressions with citation support.

Conclusion: This study shows that retrieval-augmented multimodal approaches improve factual grounding, interpretability, and reliability in radiology report generation, making them suitable for clinical support systems.

Abstract: Automated radiology report generation has gained increasing attention with the rise of deep learning and large language models. However, fully generative approaches often suffer from hallucinations and lack clinical grounding, limiting their reliability in real-world workflows. In this study, we propose a multimodal retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) system for grounded drafting of chest radiograph impressions. The system combines contrastive image-text embeddings, case-based similarity retrieval, and citation-constrained draft generation to ensure factual alignment with historical radiology reports. A curated subset of the MIMIC-CXR dataset was used to construct a multimodal retrieval database. Image embeddings were generated using CLIP encoders, while textual embeddings were derived from structured impression sections. A fusion similarity framework was implemented using FAISS indexing for scalable nearest-neighbor retrieval. Retrieved cases were used to construct grounded prompts for draft impression generation, with safety mechanisms enforcing citation coverage and confidence-based refusal. Experimental results demonstrate that multimodal fusion significantly improves retrieval performance compared to image-only retrieval, achieving Recall@5 above 0.95 on clinically relevant findings. The grounded drafting pipeline produces interpretable outputs with explicit citation traceability, enabling improved trustworthiness compared to conventional generative approaches. This work highlights the potential of retrieval-augmented multimodal systems for reliable clinical decision support and radiology workflow augmentation

</details>


### [365] [Mathematical Modeling of Cancer-Bacterial Therapy: Analysis and Numerical Simulation via Physics-Informed Neural Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.19326)
*Ayoub Farkane,David Lassounon*

Main category: q-bio.QM

TL;DR: This paper introduces a mathematical model and uses a physics-informed neural network (PINN) to study bacterial cancer therapy, focusing on the interplay among tumor growth, bacterial colonization, and oxygen levels.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The study aims to better understand the interactions among tumor growth, bacterial colonization, oxygen dynamics, and therapy efficacy to optimize bacterial cancer treatment.

Method: The paper develops a mathematical model with five reaction-diffusion equations, proves model stability, and employs a PINN for efficient simulations without meshes or large datasets, achieving robust error bounds for predictions.

Result: The model provides insights into the importance of tumor hypoxia for bacterial therapy effectiveness and shows that oxygen-tolerant bacteria may enhance treatment outcomes.

Conclusion: Sustaining hypoxia in tumors or using oxygen-tolerant bacteria could improve the efficacy of bacterial cancer therapies.

Abstract: Bacterial cancer therapy exploits anaerobic bacteria's ability to target hypoxia tumor regions, yet the interactions among tumor growth, bacterial colonization, oxygen levels, immunosuppressive cytokines, and bacterial communication remain poorly quantified. We present a mathematical model of five coupled nonlinear reaction-diffusion equations in a two-dimensional tissue domain. We proved the global well-posedness of the model and identified its steady states to analyze stability. Furthermore, a physics-informed neural network (PINN) solves the system without a mesh and without requiring extensive data. It provides convergence guarantees by combining residual stability and Sobolev approximation error bounds. This results in an overall error rate of O(n^-2 ln^4(n) + N^-1/2), which depends on the network width n and the number of collocation points N. We conducted several numerical experiments, including predicting the tumor's response to therapy. We also performed a sensitivity analysis of certain parameters. The results suggest that long-term therapeutic efficacy may require the maintenance of hypoxia regions in the tumor, or using bacteria that tolerate oxygen better, may be necessary for long-lasting tumor control.

</details>


<div id='cs.CY'></div>

# cs.CY [[Back]](#toc)

### [366] [How Motivation Relates to Generative AI Use: A Large-Scale Survey of Mexican High School Students](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.19263)
*Echo Zexuan Pan,Danny Glick,Ying Xu*

Main category: cs.CY

TL;DR: The paper explores how motivational profiles among high school students affect their use of generative AI in math and writing.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To investigate how motivational factors influence the usage of generative AI tools in educational settings, specifically focusing on math and writing.

Method: A survey was conducted among 6,793 Mexican high school students, followed by K-means clustering to identify motivational profiles based on self-concept and perceived subject value.

Result: Three distinct motivational profiles were discovered, each showing different patterns of domain-specific AI tool usage.

Conclusion: The study suggests that educational approaches integrating AI should account for students' motivational profiles rather than applying the same methods universally.

Abstract: This study examined how high school students with different motivational profiles use generative AI tools in math and writing. Through K-means clustering analysis of survey data from 6,793 Mexican high school students, we identified three distinct motivational profiles based on self-concept and perceived subject value. Results revealed distinct domain-specific AI usage patterns across students with different motivational profiles. Our findings challenge one-size-fits-all AI integration approaches and advocate for motivationally-informed educational interventions.

</details>


### [367] [Overreliance on AI in Information-seeking from Video Content](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.19843)
*Anders Giovanni Møller,Elisa Bassignana,Francesco Pierri,Luca Maria Aiello*

Main category: cs.CY

TL;DR: This study explored the effects of generative AI assistance on multimedia information-seeking tasks using 900 participants and over 8,000 video-based tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The research aimed to understand the impact of generative AI inaccuracies and vulnerabilities during video-based information retrieval tasks.

Method: The study divided participants into three groups testing variations of video access: (1) videos only, (2) videos with AI assistance, and (3) videos with deceiving AI providing false answers.

Result: AI improved accuracy where participants did not view relevant videos and enhanced efficiency, but inaccuracies in deceiving AI reduced overall accuracy by 32%, while confidence levels remained unchanged.

Conclusion: AI assistance can boost accuracy and efficiency but poses significant risks due to over-reliance and stable confidence despite errors.

Abstract: The ubiquity of multimedia content is reshaping online information spaces, particularly in social media environments. At the same time, search is being rapidly transformed by generative AI, with large language models (LLMs) routinely deployed as intermediaries between users and multimedia content to retrieve and summarize information. Despite their growing influence, the impact of LLM inaccuracies and potential vulnerabilities on multimedia information-seeking tasks remains largely unexplored. We investigate how generative AI affects accuracy, efficiency, and confidence in information retrieval from videos. We conduct an experiment with around 900 participants on 8,000+ video-based information-seeking tasks, comparing behavior across three conditions: (1) access to videos only, (2) access to videos with LLM-based AI assistance, and (3) access to videos with a deceiving AI assistant designed to provide false answers. We find that AI assistance increases accuracy by 3-7% when participants viewed the relevant video segment, and by 27-35% when they did not. Efficiency increases by 10% for short videos and 25% for longer ones. However, participants tend to over-rely on AI outputs, resulting in accuracy drops of up to 32% when interacting with the deceiving AI. Alarmingly, self-reported confidence in answers remains stable across all three conditions. Our findings expose fundamental safety risks in AI-mediated video information retrieval.

</details>


### [368] [Depictions of Depression in Generative AI Video Models: A Preliminary Study of OpenAI's Sora 2](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.19527)
*Matthew Flathers,Griffin Smith,Julian Herpertz,Zhitong Zhou,John Torous*

Main category: cs.CY

TL;DR: The study examines how OpenAI's Sora 2 generative video model represents depression in videos generated through its consumer App and developer API, highlighting differences in narrative and visual features.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To explore and understand the cultural representations of mental health conditions like depression in generative video models and how these representations vary between different access platforms.

Method: A total of 100 videos were generated using the prompt "Depression" via the Sora 2 consumer App (n=50) and developer API (n=50), followed by manual and computational analysis of narrative, visuals, and dynamics.

Result: App videos largely depicted recovery arcs with brighter visuals and more motion, while API outputs were less positive. Both platforms showed recurrent cultural symbols like hoodies, windows, and rain, and generally portrayed young adults, predominantly alone.

Conclusion: AI-generated mental health video models reflect cultural conventions and platform constraints rather than clinical validity. Clinicians should be cautious of such content influencing vulnerable populations.

Abstract: Generative video models are increasingly capable of producing complex depictions of mental health experiences, yet little is known about how these systems represent conditions like depression. This study characterizes how OpenAI's Sora 2 generative video model depicts depression and examines whether depictions differ between the consumer App and developer API access points. We generated 100 videos using the single-word prompt "Depression" across two access points: the consumer App (n=50) and developer API (n=50). Two trained coders independently coded narrative structure, visual environments, objects, figure demographics, and figure states. Computational features across visual aesthetics, audio, semantic content, and temporal dynamics were extracted and compared between modalities. App-generated videos exhibited a pronounced recovery bias: 78% (39/50) featured narrative arcs progressing from depressive states toward resolution, compared with 14% (7/50) of API outputs. App videos brightened over time (slope = 2.90 brightness units/second vs. -0.18 for API; d = 1.59, q < .001) and contained three times more motion (d = 2.07, q < .001). Across both modalities, videos converged on a narrow visual vocabulary and featured recurring objects including hoodies (n=194), windows (n=148), and rain (n=83). Figures were predominantly young adults (88% aged 20-30) and nearly always alone (98%). Gender varied by access point: App outputs skewed male (68%), API outputs skewed female (59%). Sora 2 does not invent new visual grammars for depression but compresses and recombines cultural iconographies, while platform-level constraints substantially shape which narratives reach users. Clinicians should be aware that AI-generated mental health video content reflects training data and platform design rather than clinical knowledge, and that patients may encounter such content during vulnerable periods.

</details>


### [369] [Plagiarism or Productivity? Students Moral Disengagement and Behavioral Intentions to Use ChatGPT in Academic Writing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.19549)
*John Paul P. Miranda,Rhiziel P. Manalese,Mark Anthony A. Castro,Renen Paul M. Viado,Vernon Grace M. Maniago,Rudante M. Galapon,Jovita G. Rivera,Amado B. Martinez*

Main category: cs.CY

TL;DR: The study explored how moral disengagement impacts Filipino college students' intention to use ChatGPT in academic writing, finding that attribution of blame significantly influences attitudes which, in turn, drive behavioral intention.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand the ethical and psychological factors driving students' use of AI tools like ChatGPT in an academic context, focusing on mechanisms of moral disengagement.

Method: 418 Filipino students familiar with ChatGPT participated in a survey-based model testing five mechanisms of moral disengagement and their impact on attitudes, subjective norms, perceived behavioral control, and behavioral intention.

Result: Attribution of blame had the strongest impact on attitudes, and attitudes significantly influenced behavioral intention. The model explained over half the variation in students’ intentions to use ChatGPT.

Conclusion: Clear academic integrity policies and ethical guidelines are necessary as students leverage institutional gaps to justify AI use. Intention-based models are insufficient; emotional and peer factors also play a critical role.

Abstract: This study examined how moral disengagement influences Filipino college students' intention to use ChatGPT in academic writing. The model tested five mechanisms: moral justification, euphemistic labeling, displacement of responsibility, minimizing consequences, and attribution of blame. These mechanisms were analyzed as predictors of attitudes, subjective norms, and perceived behavioral control, which then predicted behavioral intention. A total of 418 students with ChatGPT experience participated. The results showed that several moral disengagement mechanisms influenced students' attitudes and sense of control. Among the predictors, attribution of blame had the strongest influence, while attitudes had the highest impact on behavioral intention. The model explained more than half of the variation in intention. These results suggest that students often rely on institutional gaps and peer behavior to justify AI use. Many believe it is acceptable to use ChatGPT for learning or when rules are unclear. This shows a need for clear academic integrity policies, ethical guidance, and classroom support. The study also recognizes that intention-based models may not fully explain student behavior. Emotional factors, peer influence, and convenience can also affect decisions. The results provide useful insights for schools that aim to support responsible and informed AI use in higher education.

</details>


<div id='hep-ex'></div>

# hep-ex [[Back]](#toc)

### [370] [AI Agents Can Already Autonomously Perform Experimental High Energy Physics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.20179)
*Eric A. Moreno,Samuel Bright-Thonney,Andrzej Novak,Dolores Garcia,Philip Harris*

Main category: hep-ex

TL;DR: The paper showcases an AI agent framework that autonomously handles high-energy physics (HEP) analysis pipelines using datasets, an execution framework, and literature. The authors argue that current capabilities of such agents are underestimated by the HEP community.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The authors aim to highlight the potential of AI agents in reducing the manual workload in HEP analyses by arguing that existing approaches and workflows overly constrain the capabilities of AI.

Method: The work uses Claude Code to automate major HEP analysis steps (event selection, background estimation, etc.) within their Just Furnish Context (JFC) framework, integrating literature-based knowledge retrieval and multi-agent reviews.

Result: The JFC framework successfully demonstrated analyses across multiple HEP datasets, achieving credible outcomes in electroweak, QCD, and Higgs boson measurements.

Conclusion: The paper suggests that integrating AI agents can free physicists from mundane tasks, allowing focus on innovative and rigorous aspects of research, and calls for changes in community training and resource allocation strategies.

Abstract: Large language model-based AI agents are now able to autonomously execute substantial portions of a high energy physics (HEP) analysis pipeline with minimal expert-curated input. Given access to a HEP dataset, an execution framework, and a corpus of prior experimental literature, we find that Claude Code succeeds in automating all stages of a typical analysis: event selection, background estimation, uncertainty quantification, statistical inference, and paper drafting. We argue that the experimental HEP community is underestimating the current capabilities of these systems, and that most proposed agentic workflows are too narrowly scoped or scaffolded to specific analysis structures. We present a proof-of-concept framework, Just Furnish Context (JFC), that integrates autonomous analysis agents with literature-based knowledge retrieval and multi-agent review, and show that this is sufficient to plan, execute, and document a credible high energy physics analysis. We demonstrate this by conducting analyses on open data from ALEPH, DELPHI, and CMS to perform electroweak, QCD, and Higgs boson measurements. Rather than replacing physicists, these tools promise to offload the repetitive technical burden of analysis code development, freeing researchers to focus on physics insight, truly novel method development, and rigorous validation. Given these developments, we advocate for new strategies for how the community trains students, organizes analysis efforts, and allocates human expertise.

</details>


<div id='cs.CR'></div>

# cs.CR [[Back]](#toc)

### [371] [Benchmarking Post-Quantum Cryptography on Resource-Constrained IoT Devices: ML-KEM and ML-DSA on ARM Cortex-M0+](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.19340)
*Rojin Chhetri*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: This paper benchmarks the performance of finalized NIST post-quantum cryptography standards ML-KEM and ML-DSA on an ARM Cortex-M0+ processor and provides key findings on energy and latency efficiencies.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The shift to post-quantum cryptography is essential for long-lived IoT devices, yet there are no performance benchmarks for finalized NIST standards on the resource-constrained 32-bit processor class.

Method: The authors use PQClean reference C implementations to measure the performance of ML-KEM and ML-DSA at various security levels on the ARM Cortex-M0+ processor. They evaluate metrics like latency, energy consumption, and reproducibility on the RP2040 platform.

Result: ML-KEM-512 achieves a 17x energy efficiency improvement over ECDH P-256. However, ML-DSA signing exhibits high latency variance due to rejection sampling. The M0+ processor's performance is only 1.8-1.9x slower than Cortex-M4 benchmarks, despite hardware limitations.

Conclusion: The study demonstrates significant energy efficiency and performance insights for implementing NIST post-quantum cryptography on constrained devices like the Cortex-M0+, and provides openly available benchmark data for future research.

Abstract: The migration to post-quantum cryptography is urgent for Internet of Things devices with 10-20 year lifespans, yet no systematic benchmarks exist for the finalised NIST standards on the most constrained 32-bit processor class. This paper presents the first isolated algorithm-level benchmarks of ML-KEM (FIPS 203) and ML-DSA (FIPS 204) on ARM Cortex-M0+, measured on the RP2040 (Raspberry Pi Pico) at 133 MHz with 264 KB SRAM. Using PQClean reference C implementations, we measure all three security levels of ML-KEM (512/768/1024) and ML-DSA (44/65/87) across key generation, encapsulation/signing, and decapsulation/verification. ML-KEM-512 completes a full key exchange in 36.3 ms consuming 2.87 mJ--17x faster and 94% less energy than ECDH P-256 on the same hardware. ML-DSA signing exhibits high latency variance due to rejection sampling (coefficient of variation 61-71%, 99th-percentile up to 1,115 ms for ML-DSA-87). The M0+ incurs only a 1.8-1.9x slowdown relative to published Cortex-M4 results, despite lacking 64-bit multiply, DSP, and SIMD instructions. All code, data, and scripts are released as an open-source benchmark suite for reproducibility.

</details>


### [372] [Case Study: Horizontal Side-Channel Analysis Attack against Elliptic Curve Scalar Multiplication Accelerator under Laser Illumination](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.19811)
*Dmytro Petryk,Ievgen Kabin,Peter Langendoerfer,Zoya Dyka*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: This paper explores the vulnerability of elliptic curve scalar multiplication accelerators to combined side-channel and laser illumination attacks, showcasing the potential impact on cryptographic chips.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To investigate the susceptibility of cryptographic hardware to combined physical attacks, particularly Side-Channel Analysis (SCA) and laser illumination.

Method: Conduct combined SCA and laser illumination attacks using a Teledyne LeCroy differential probe to analyze the static and dynamic power consumption of an elliptic curve scalar multiplication accelerator.

Result: Laser illumination increased power consumption, especially static power, but had an insignificant effect on horizontal power analysis attack success. The authors hypothesize that stronger lasers and static current-focused measurements could elevate attack effectiveness.

Conclusion: Static Consumption under Laser Illumination (SCuLI attacks) represent a novel, untested threat to cryptographic chips, particularly those in scaled technologies. Further study is required to evaluate their feasibility and identify necessary countermeasures.

Abstract: Devices employing cryptographic approaches have to be resistant to physical attacks. Side-Channel Analysis (SCA) and Fault Injection (FI) attacks are frequently used to reveal cryptographic keys. In this paper, we present a combined SCA and laser illumination attack against an Elliptic Curve Scalar Multiplication accelerator using a differential probe from Teledyne LeCroy. Our experiments show that laser illumination increases the power consumption of the chip, especially its static power consumption but the success of the horizontal power analysis attacks was changed insignificantly. We assume that using a laser with a high laser beam power and concentrating on measuring and analysing only static current can improve the attack success significantly. The horizontal attacks against public key cryptosystems exploiting the Static Consumption under Laser Illumination (SCuLI attacks) are novel and their potential is not investigated yet. These attacks can be especially dangerous against cryptographic chips manufactured in scaled technologies. If such attacks are feasible, appropriate countermeasures have to be proposed in the future.

</details>


### [373] [Kumo: A Security-Focused Serverless Cloud Simulator](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.19787)
*Wei Shao,Khaled Khasawneh,Setareh Rafatirad,Houman Homayoun,Chongzhou Fang*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: The paper introduces Kumo, a simulator designed to explore security risks in serverless platforms, and highlights the importance of distinguishing between scheduler-driven risks and resource exhaustion vulnerabilities.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to understand and analyze security risks in serverless computing, particularly issues arising from shared execution environments, which existing tools do not adequately address.

Method: The authors developed Kumo, a discrete-event simulator modeling scheduling, container reuse, resource contention, and more. Kumo explicitly includes attacker and victim entities to evaluate security metrics like co-location probability and denial-of-service impacts.

Result: The study found that scheduler decisions significantly influence co-location risks, while factors like queuing policies and system capacity primarily govern denial-of-service vulnerabilities.

Conclusion: Kumo provides a structured approach to study security issues in serverless platforms, emphasizing the need for security-aware scheduling strategies and distinguishing between different types of risks.

Abstract: Serverless computing abstracts infrastructure management but also obscures system-level behaviors that can introduce security risks. Prior work has shown that serverless platforms are vulnerable to attacks exploiting shared execution environments, including attacker--victim co-location and denial-of-service through resource contention, yet analyzing these risks on production platforms is difficult due to limited observability, high cost, and lack of experimental control, while existing simulators primarily focus on performance and cost rather than security. We present Kumo, a security-focused simulator for serverless platforms that enables controlled, reproducible analysis of security risks arising from scheduling and resource sharing decisions. Kumo models invocation arrivals, scheduler placement, container reuse, resource contention, and queuing within a discrete-event framework, explicitly representing attackers and victims as first-class entities and providing metrics such as co-location probability, time to first co-location, invocation drop rate, and tail latency. Through two case studies, we show that scheduler choice is a first-order factor for co-location attacks, inducing orders-of-magnitude differences under identical workloads, while Denial-of-Service behavior is largely governed by system-level factors such as service time, queuing policy, and cluster capacity once contention dominates. These results highlight the need to distinguish scheduler-driven isolation risks from broader resource exhaustion vulnerabilities and position Kumo as a flexible foundation for systematic, security-aware exploration of serverless platforms.

</details>


### [374] [A Novel Solution for Zero-Day Attack Detection in IDS using Self-Attention and Jensen-Shannon Divergence in WGAN-GP](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.19350)
*Ziyu Mu,Xiyu Shi,Safak Dogan*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: The paper addresses zero-day attack challenges in cybersecurity by introducing SA-WGAN-GP, JS-WGAN-GP, and SA-JS-WGAN-GP to improve IDS performance via synthetic network traffic generation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome the challenge of detecting zero-day attacks, which exploit unknown vulnerabilities and are difficult to defend against using traditional methods.

Method: The paper proposes novel GAN-based models (SA-WGAN-GP, JS-WGAN-GP, and SA-JS-WGAN-GP) incorporating advanced mechanisms like self-attention and JS divergence for enhanced data generation and IDS training.

Result: Evaluation on the NSL-KDD dataset using the leave-one-attack-type-out method shows that the proposed GAN-based models enhance IDS performance and zero-day attack detection.

Conclusion: The integration of self-attention and JS divergence into WGAN-GP models significantly improves the IDS's ability to detect zero-day attacks through better data augmentation.

Abstract: The increasing sophistication of cyber threats, especially zero-day attacks, poses a significant challenge to cybersecurity. Zero-day attacks exploit unknown vulnerabilities, making them difficult to detect and defend against. Existing approaches patch flaws and deploy an Intrusion Detection System (IDS). Using advanced Wasserstein GANs with Gradient Penalty (WGAN-GP), this paper makes a novel proposition to synthesize network traffic that mimics zero-day patterns, enriching data diversity and improving IDS generalization. SA-WGAN-GP is first introduced, which adds a Self-Attention (SA) mechanism to capture long-range cross-feature dependencies by reshaping the feature vector into tokens after dense projections. A JS-WGAN-GP is then proposed, which adds a Jensen-Shannon (JS) divergence-based auxiliary discriminator that is trained with Binary Cross-Entropy (BCE), frozen during updates, and used to regularize the generator for smoother gradients and higher sample quality. Third, SA-JS-WGAN-GP is created by combining the SA mechanism with JS divergence, thereby enhancing the data generation ability of WGAN-GP. As data augmentation does not equate with true zero-day attack discovery, we emulate zero-day attacks via the leave-one-attack-type-out method on the NSL-KDD dataset for training all GANs and IDS models in the assessment of the effectiveness of the proposed solution. The evaluation results show that integrating SA and JS divergence into WGAN-GP yields superior IDS performance and more effective zero-day risk detection.

</details>


### [375] [The Autonomy Tax: Defense Training Breaks LLM Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.19423)
*Shawn Li,Yue Zhao*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: Defense training for large language model (LLM) agents undermines their tool-execution abilities in multi-step tasks, while failing to guard against sophisticated attacks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper aims to address the challenge of securing LLM agents from prompt injection attacks, which manipulate model behavior during multi-step tool-based tasks.

Method: The authors conducted evaluations comparing defense-trained and undefended models across 97 tasks and 1,000 adversarial prompts, identifying systematic biases and their root causes.

Result: Defended models displayed incompetence, propagated task failures, and degraded security performance, with bias stemming from shortcut learning rather than semantic threat understanding.

Conclusion: Existing defense methods compromise agent capability while failing to provide substantive security, calling for new strategies that balance safety and competence.

Abstract: Large language model (LLM) agents increasingly rely on external tools (file operations, API calls, database transactions) to autonomously complete complex multi-step tasks. Practitioners deploy defense-trained models to protect against prompt injection attacks that manipulate agent behavior through malicious observations or retrieved content. We reveal a fundamental \textbf{capability-alignment paradox}: defense training designed to improve safety systematically destroys agent competence while failing to prevent sophisticated attacks. Evaluating defended models against undefended baselines across 97 agent tasks and 1,000 adversarial prompts, we uncover three systematic biases unique to multi-step agents. \textbf{Agent incompetence bias} manifests as immediate tool execution breakdown, with models refusing or generating invalid actions on benign tasks before observing any external content. \textbf{Cascade amplification bias} causes early failures to propagate through retry loops, pushing defended models to timeout on 99\% of tasks compared to 13\% for baselines. \textbf{Trigger bias} leads to paradoxical security degradation where defended models perform worse than undefended baselines while straightforward attacks bypass defenses at high rates. Root cause analysis reveals these biases stem from shortcut learning: models overfit to surface attack patterns rather than semantic threat understanding, evidenced by extreme variance in defense effectiveness across attack categories. Our findings demonstrate that current defense paradigms optimize for single-turn refusal benchmarks while rendering multi-step agents fundamentally unreliable, necessitating new approaches that preserve tool execution competence under adversarial conditions.

</details>


### [376] [A Framework for Formalizing LLM Agent Security](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.19469)
*Vincent Siu,Jingxuan He,Kyle Montgomery,Zhun Wang,Neil Gong,Chenguang Wang,Dawn Song*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: The paper focuses on contextual security in LLM agents, proposing a framework to systematize attacks and defenses through four security properties: task alignment, action alignment, source authorization, and data isolation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current security approaches for LLM agents often fail to address the contextual nature of actions, leading to a tradeoff between utility and security effectiveness.

Method: It introduces four security properties capturing contextual security and utilizes oracle functions for verifying property violations to reformulate attacks and defenses.

Result: The framework offers precise, context-aware definitions for attacks like prompt injection, jailbreak, and memory poisoning and strengthens defenses via oracle functions.

Conclusion: This work lays the groundwork for improving security measures in LLM agents by contextualizing attacks and defenses, enabling future research in tailored security approaches.

Abstract: Security in LLM agents is inherently contextual. For example, the same action taken by an agent may represent legitimate behavior or a security violation depending on whose instruction led to the action, what objective is being pursued, and whether the action serves that objective. However, existing definitions of security attacks against LLM agents often fail to capture this contextual nature. As a result, defenses face a fundamental utility-security tradeoff: applying defenses uniformly across all contexts can lead to significant utility loss, while applying defenses in insufficient or inappropriate contexts can result in security vulnerabilities. In this work, we present a framework that systematizes existing attacks and defenses from the perspective of contextual security. To this end, we propose four security properties that capture contextual security for LLM agents: task alignment (pursuing authorized objectives), action alignment (individual actions serving those objectives), source authorization (executing commands from authenticated sources), and data isolation (ensuring information flows respect privilege boundaries). We further introduce a set of oracle functions that enable verification of whether these security properties are violated as an agent executes a user task. Using this framework, we reformalize existing attacks, such as indirect prompt injection, direct prompt injection, jailbreak, task drift, and memory poisoning, as violations of one or more security properties, thereby providing precise and contextual definitions of these attacks. Similarly, we reformalize defenses as mechanisms that strengthen oracle functions or perform security property checks. Finally, we discuss several important future research directions enabled by our framework.

</details>


### [377] [Automated Membership Inference Attacks: Discovering MIA Signal Computations using LLM Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.19375)
*Toan Tran,Olivera Kotevska,Li Xiong*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: The paper introduces AutoMIA, a framework utilizing large language model agents to automate the design of membership inference attacks (MIAs), achieving improved attack strategies and state-of-the-art performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Designing MIAs typically requires manual exploration to identify vulnerabilities in machine learning models, making the process challenging and limited in scope.

Method: The authors use large language model agents within the AutoMIA framework to systematically explore attack strategies and automate the generation of novel MIA signals.

Result: AutoMIA discovers tailored MIAs for specific models and datasets, achieving absolute AUC improvements of up to 0.18 over existing approaches.

Conclusion: AutoMIA demonstrates the scalability and effectiveness of using LLM agents for designing state-of-the-art MIAs, highlighting potential for further exploration in automated security assessments of machine learning systems.

Abstract: Membership inference attacks (MIAs), which enable adversaries to determine whether specific data points were part of a model's training dataset, have emerged as an important framework to understand, assess, and quantify the potential information leakage associated with machine learning systems. Designing effective MIAs is a challenging task that usually requires extensive manual exploration of model behaviors to identify potential vulnerabilities. In this paper, we introduce AutoMIA -- a novel framework that leverages large language model (LLM) agents to automate the design and implementation of new MIA signal computations. By utilizing LLM agents, we can systematically explore a vast space of potential attack strategies, enabling the discovery of novel strategies. Our experiments demonstrate AutoMIA can successfully discover new MIAs that are specifically tailored to user-configured target model and dataset, resulting in improvements of up to 0.18 in absolute AUC over existing MIAs. This work provides the first demonstration that LLM agents can serve as an effective and scalable paradigm for designing and implementing MIAs with SOTA performance, opening up new avenues for future exploration.

</details>


### [378] [Trojan's Whisper: Stealthy Manipulation of OpenClaw through Injected Bootstrapped Guidance](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.19974)
*Fazhong Liu,Zhuoyan Chen,Tu Lan,Haozhen Tan,Zhenyu Xu,Xiang Li,Guoxing Chen,Yan Meng,Haojin Zhu*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: Autonomous coding agents, while enhancing automation, face risks from a new attack vector called guidance injection. This stealthy method embeds harmful operational narratives into an agent's reasoning, enabling adversarial actions without detection.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To explore the vulnerabilities within autonomous coding agents, specifically identifying how their extensible skill ecosystems might be exploited by adversarial forces.

Method: The researchers created 26 malicious skills across 13 attack categories and evaluated their impact using ORE-Bench, a developer workspace benchmark. They tested attacks across 52 prompts and six LLM backends.

Result: The attacks achieved success rates of 16.0% to 64.2%. Most malicious actions were executed autonomously without user confirmation, and 94% evaded detection by current scanners.

Conclusion: Autonomous agent ecosystems face design tensions that make them vulnerable to guidance injection attacks, highlighting the pressing need for capability isolation, runtime policy enforcement, and transparent provenance systems.

Abstract: Autonomous coding agents are increasingly integrated into software development workflows, offering capabilities that extend beyond code suggestion to active system interaction and environment management. OpenClaw, a representative platform in this emerging paradigm, introduces an extensible skill ecosystem that allows third-party developers to inject behavioral guidance through lifecycle hooks during agent initialization. While this design enhances automation and customization, it also opens a novel and unexplored attack surface. In this paper, we identify and systematically characterize guidance injection, a stealthy attack vector that embeds adversarial operational narratives into bootstrap guidance files. Unlike traditional prompt injection, which relies on explicit malicious instructions, guidance injection manipulates the agent's reasoning context by framing harmful actions as routine best practices. These narratives are automatically incorporated into the agent's interpretive framework and influence future task execution without raising suspicion.We construct 26 malicious skills spanning 13 attack categories including credential exfiltration, workspace destruction, privilege escalation, and persistent backdoor installation. We evaluate them using ORE-Bench, a realistic developer workspace benchmark we developed. Across 52 natural user prompts and six state-of-the-art LLM backends, our attacks achieve success rates from 16.0% to 64.2%, with the majority of malicious actions executed autonomously without user confirmation. Furthermore, 94% of our malicious skills evade detection by existing static and LLM-based scanners. Our findings reveal fundamental tensions in the design of autonomous agent ecosystems and underscore the urgent need for defenses based on capability isolation, runtime policy enforcement, and transparent guidance provenance.

</details>


### [379] [TAPAS: Efficient Two-Server Asymmetric Private Aggregation Beyond Prio(+)](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.19949)
*Harish Karthikeyan,Antigoni Polychroniadou*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: The paper introduces TAPAS, a two-server privacy-preserving aggregation scheme that optimizes costs, enhances security, and supports modern high-dimensional AI tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing two-server protocols for private aggregation are limited by their symmetric costs and scalability challenges with high-dimensional data used in modern AI tasks.

Method: TAPAS employs an asymmetric approach where one server handles computation-intensive tasks, while the other performs lightweight facilitation. It incorporates lattice-based cryptography and novel zero-knowledge proofs for enhanced security and robustness.

Result: TAPAS achieves server costs independent of input dimensionality for one server, post-quantum security, and full malicious security for the servers, making it suitable for large-dimensional AI applications.

Conclusion: TAPAS sets a new standard for secure, scalable, and efficient privacy-preserving aggregation, especially for federated learning and telemetry, using innovative cryptographic techniques.

Abstract: Privacy-preserving aggregation is a cornerstone for AI systems that learn from distributed data without exposing individual records, especially in federated learning and telemetry. Existing two-server protocols (e.g., Prio and successors) set a practical baseline by validating inputs while preventing any single party from learning users' values, but they impose symmetric costs on both servers and communication that scales with the per-client input dimension $L$. Modern learning tasks routinely involve dimensionalities $L$ in the tens to hundreds of millions of model parameters.
  We present TAPAS, a two-server asymmetric private aggregation scheme that addresses these limitations along four dimensions: (i) no trusted setup or preprocessing, (ii) server-side communication that is independent of $L$ (iii) post-quantum security based solely on standard lattice assumptions (LWE, SIS), and (iv) stronger robustness with identifiable abort and full malicious security for the servers. A key design choice is intentional asymmetry: one server bears the $O(L)$ aggregation and verification work, while the other operates as a lightweight facilitator with computation independent of $L$. This reduces total cost, enables the secondary server to run on commodity hardware, and strengthens the non-collusion assumption of the servers. One of our main contributions is a suite of new and efficient lattice-based zero-knowledge proofs; to our knowledge, we are the first to establish privacy and correctness with identifiable abort in the two-server setting.

</details>


### [380] [Channel Prediction-Based Physical Layer Authentication under Consecutive Spoofing Attacks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.19962)
*Yijia Guo,Junqing Zhang,Yao-Win Peter Hong*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: This paper proposes a Transformer-based channel prediction framework for physical layer authentication (PLA) in wireless networks, addressing consecutive spoofing attacks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing PLA methods struggle under consecutive spoofing attacks due to channel changes from mobility and fading.

Method: The approach uses a Transformer-based module to predict legitimate channel state information (CSI) during spoofing and dynamically updates its inputs based on authentication decisions.

Result: Simulations show reduced error in channel prediction and higher authentication accuracy compared to benchmarks, remaining robust against prolonged spoofing attacks.

Conclusion: The framework effectively enhances authentication in wireless networks prone to sustained spoofing, outperforming traditional methods.

Abstract: Wireless networks are highly vulnerable to spoofing attacks, especially when attackers transmit consecutive spoofing packets. Conventional physical layer authentication (PLA) methods have mostly focused on single-packet spoofing attack. However, under consecutive spoofing attacks, they become ineffective due to channel evolution caused by device mobility and channel fading. To address this challenge, we propose a channel prediction-based PLA framework. Specifically, a Transformer-based channel prediction module is employed to predict legitimate CSI measurements during spoofing interval, and the input of channel prediction module is adaptively updated with predicted or observed CSI measurements based on the authentication decision to ensure robustness against sustained spoofing. Simulation results under Rayleigh fading channels demonstrate that the proposed approach achieves low prediction error and significantly higher authentication accuracy than conventional benchmark, maintaining robustness even under extended spoofing attacks.

</details>


### [381] [Evolving Jailbreaks: Automated Multi-Objective Long-Tail Attacks on Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.20122)
*Wenjing Hong,Zhonghua Rong,Li Wang,Feng Chang,Jian Zhu,Ke Tang,Zexuan Zhu,Yew-Soon Ong*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: EvoJail introduces an automated framework utilizing evolutionary search to efficiently discover jailbreak attack prompts targeting large language models (LLMs), addressing vulnerabilities in long-tail distributions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation behind this paper is to address security risks posed by jailbreak attacks on LLMs, particularly in scenarios involving diverse long-tail user inputs such as low-resource languages or encrypted data.

Method: The proposed EvoJail framework leverages multi-objective evolutionary search, focusing on jointly improving attack performance and reducing output perplexity, using semantic-algorithmic representation and LLM-assisted mutation and crossover.

Result: EvoJail discovers diverse and effective jailbreak strategies, demonstrating competitive performance against existing methods in both targeted and ensemble approaches.

Conclusion: EvoJail successfully provides a systematic way to identify long-tail distribution vulnerabilities, enhancing our understanding and mitigation strategies for jailbreak attacks on LLMs.

Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) have been widely deployed, especially through free Web-based applications that expose them to diverse user-generated inputs, including those from long-tail distributions such as low-resource languages and encrypted private data. This open-ended exposure increases the risk of jailbreak attacks that undermine model safety alignment. While recent studies have shown that leveraging long-tail distributions can facilitate such jailbreaks, existing approaches largely rely on handcrafted rules, limiting the systematic evaluation of these security and privacy vulnerabilities. In this work, we present EvoJail, an automated framework for discovering long-tail distribution attacks via multi-objective evolutionary search. EvoJail formulates long-tail attack prompt generation as a multi-objective optimization problem that jointly maximizes attack effectiveness and minimizes output perplexity, and introduces a semantic-algorithmic solution representation to capture both high-level semantic intent and low-level structural transformations of encryption-decryption logic. Building upon this representation, EvoJail integrates LLM-assisted operators into a multi-objective evolutionary framework, enabling adaptive and semantically informed mutation and crossover for efficiently exploring a highly structured and open-ended search space. Extensive experiments demonstrate that EvoJail consistently discovers diverse and effective long-tail jailbreak strategies, achieving competitive performance with existing methods in both individual and ensemble level.

</details>


### [382] [Improving Generalization on Cybersecurity Tasks with Multi-Modal Contrastive Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.20181)
*Jianan Huang,Rodolfo V. Valentim,Luca Vassio,Matteo Boffa,Marco Mellia,Idilio Drago,Dario Rossi*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: The paper explores multi-modal contrastive learning to address generalization issues in using ML for cybersecurity, particularly for threat classification.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing cybersecurity ML models struggle to generalize well in real-world production due to learning superficial patterns instead of core cybersecurity concepts.

Method: The authors propose a two-stage multi-modal contrastive learning approach. First, they create a semantically meaningful embedding space using textual vulnerability descriptions and then align payload data to this space to transfer knowledge.

Result: The framework improves performance and reduces shortcut learning compared to baselines. It is tested on both private and synthetic public datasets, demonstrating improved generalization.

Conclusion: Multi-modal contrastive learning shows promise in addressing generalization issues in ML-driven cybersecurity systems. The synthetic benchmark and open-source code are shared to encourage further research.

Abstract: The use of ML in cybersecurity has long been impaired by generalization issues: Models that work well in controlled scenarios fail to maintain performance in production. The root cause often lies in ML algorithms learning superficial patterns (shortcuts) rather than underlying cybersecurity concepts. We investigate contrastive multi-modal learning as a first step towards improving ML performance in cybersecurity tasks. We aim at transferring knowledge from data-rich modalities, such as text, to data-scarce modalities, such as payloads. We set up a case study on threat classification and propose a two-stage multi-modal contrastive learning framework that uses textual vulnerability descriptions to guide payload classification. First, we construct a semantically meaningful embedding space using contrastive learning on descriptions. Then, we align payloads to this space, transferring knowledge from text to payloads. We evaluate the approach on a large-scale private dataset and a synthetic benchmark built from public CVE descriptions and LLM-generated payloads. The methodology appears to reduce shortcut learning over baselines on both benchmarks. We release our synthetic benchmark and source code as open source.

</details>


<div id='math.OC'></div>

# math.OC [[Back]](#toc)

### [383] [Branched Optimal Transport for Stimulus to Reaction Brain Mapping](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.19751)
*Cristian Mendico*

Main category: math.OC

TL;DR: This paper proposes a variational framework to model how external stimuli propagate in the brain by inferring a graph-based transport network rather than assuming a fixed network.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Understanding how external stimuli propagate through the brain to produce reactions is crucial, yet current models only work on pre-defined networks without inferring the underlying transport structures.

Method: The authors propose a variational framework based on anisotropic branched optimal transport, which infers a graph/current connecting the source and target of brain reactions. They also introduce a hybrid stochastic extension incorporating Kullback--Leibler control costs.

Result: The framework provides mathematical models to infer stimulus-to-reaction propagation architectures and demonstrates the existence of minimizers for both discrete and continuous problem formulations.

Conclusion: This new approach allows for the construction of brain reaction maps, advancing the understanding of stimulus propagation by treating the transport network itself as an unknown to be inferred.

Abstract: A central problem in systems neuroscience is to determine how an external stimulation is propagated through the brain so as to produce a reaction. Current deterministic and stochastic control models quantify transition costs between brain states on a prescribed network, but do not treat the transport network itself as an unknown. Here we propose a variational framework in which the inferred object is a graph/current connecting a stimulation source measure to a reaction target measure. The model is posed as an anisotropic branched optimal transport problem, where concavity of the flux cost promotes aggregation and branching. The support of an optimal current defines a stimulus-to-reaction routing architecture, interpreted as a brain reaction map. We prove existence of minimizers in discrete and continuous formulations and introduce a hybrid stochastic extension combining ramified transport with a path-space Kullback--Leibler control cost on the induced graph dynamics. This approach provides a mathematical mechanism for inferring propagation architectures rather than controlling trajectories on fixed substrates.

</details>


### [384] [Multimodal branched transport infers anatomically aligned brain reaction maps](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.19761)
*Cristian Mendico*

Main category: math.OC

TL;DR: This paper develops a framework to infer branched propagation architectures in the brain by combining multimodal data and optimizing transmission costs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the gap in understanding how external stimulation transforms into distributed brain reaction patterns, particularly at the level of propagation architecture.

Method: The study integrates BOLD responses, electrophysiology, and tractography-derived anisotropy to infer propagation architecture using a variational optimization approach based on anatomical transport costs.

Result: The proposed method produces anatomically-aligned reaction maps, highlights the impact of anisotropic costs on routing, and uncovers geometric-dynamical trade-offs with non-trivial rank reversals in branching structures.

Conclusion: The approach offers new insights into brain connectivity and propagation mechanisms, demonstrating the value of multimodal integration and branched transport models for understanding neural dynamics.

Abstract: How external stimulation is transformed into distributed reaction patterns remains unresolved at the level of propagation architecture. Existing large-scale control models quantify transition costs on prescribed networks but do not infer the routing map itself from source and target activity. Here we combine task-related blood-oxygen-level-dependent responses, source-reconstructed electrophysiology and tractography-derived anisotropy to estimate stimulation and reaction measures, define an anatomical transport cost, and infer a branched propagation architecture by variational optimisation. Unlike standard transport formulations, branched transport favours aggregation of signal into shared neural highways before redistribution. We further attach a stochastic graph-induced dynamics to the inferred map and quantify the trade-off between geometric efficiency and dynamical controllability. We show that multimodal data generate anatomically aligned brain reaction maps, that anisotropic costs qualitatively reshape routing backbones relative to isotropic baselines, and that hybrid geometric--dynamical optimisation reveals non-trivial rank reversals across branching regimes.

</details>


### [385] [Infinite-dimensional spherical-radial decomposition for probabilistic functions, with application to constrained optimal control and Gaussian process regression](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.19907)
*Kewei Wang,Georg Stadler*

Main category: math.OC

TL;DR: The paper introduces hiSRD, an unbiased and low-variance method combining SRD and Monte Carlo for estimating probabilistic functions in infinite stochastic dimensions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Extend the spherical-radial decomposition (SRD) to handle infinite stochastic dimensions efficiently.

Method: Developed hybrid infinite-dimensional SRD (hiSRD), integrating subspace SRD with Monte Carlo methods.

Result: Theoretical analysis shows elimination of truncation bias, reduced variance, and derivative computation capabilities, supported by numerical studies.

Conclusion: hiSRD improves accuracy and efficiency in chance-constrained optimization and Gaussian process regression.

Abstract: The spherical-radial decomposition (SRD) is an efficient method for estimating probabilistic functions and their gradients defined over finite-dimensional elliptical distributions. In this work, we generalize the SRD to infinite stochastic dimensions by combining subspace SRD with standard Monte Carlo methods. The resulting method, which we call hybrid infinite-dimensional SRD (hiSRD) provides an unbiased, low-variance estimator for convex sets arising, for instance, in chance-constrained optimization. We provide a theoretical analysis of the variance of finite-dimensional SRD as the dimension increases, and show that the proposed hybrid method eliminates truncation-induced bias, reduces variance, and allows the computation of derivatives of probabilistic functions. We present comprehensive numerical studies for a risk-neutral stochastic PDE optimal control problem with joint chance state constraints, and for optimizing kernel parameters in Gaussian process regression under the constraint that the posterior process satisfies joint chance constraints.

</details>


### [386] [Structural Controllability of Large-Scale Hypergraphs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.19955)
*Joshua Pickard,Xin Mao,Can Chen*

Main category: math.OC

TL;DR: This paper develops a framework for structural controllability of hypergraphs using polynomial dynamical systems, overcoming challenges of scalability and nonlinearity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Controlling real-world systems with hypergraph dynamics remains a challenge due to nonlinearities and large scales, with most research focusing only on graph-level control and overlooking hypergraphs.

Method: The authors extend graph-based notions of accessibility and dilation to hypergraph dynamics, establish topology-based controllability criteria, derive bounds for driver nodes, and propose a scalable algorithm for driver node selection.

Result: The proposed methods are shown to be effective and scalable through numerical experiments on hypergraphs of varying sizes and complexities.

Conclusion: This work breaks ground in structural controllability for hypergraphs, establishing a scalable, topology-driven framework applicable to real-world networked systems with higher-order interactions.

Abstract: Controlling real-world networked systems, including ecological, biomedical, and engineered networks that exhibit higher-order interactions, remains challenging due to inherent nonlinearities and large system scales. Despite extensive studies on graph controllability, the controllability properties of hypergraphs remain largely underdeveloped. Existing results focus primarily on exact controllability, which is often impractical for large-scale hypergraphs. In this article, we develop a structural controllability framework for hypergraphs by modeling hypergraph dynamics as polynomial dynamical systems. In particular, we extend classical notions of accessibility and dilation from linear graph-based systems to polynomial hypergraph dynamics and establish a hypergraph-based criterion under which the topology guarantees satisfaction of classical Lie-algebraic and Kalman-type rank conditions for almost all parameter choices. We further derive a topology-based lower bound on the minimum number of driver nodes required for structural controllability and leverage this bound to design a scalable driver node selection algorithm combining dilation-aware initialization via maximum matching with greedy accessibility expansion. We demonstrate the effectiveness and scalability of the proposed framework through numerical experiments on hypergraphs with tens to thousands of nodes and higher-order interactions.

</details>


<div id='stat.ME'></div>

# stat.ME [[Back]](#toc)

### [387] [Learning to Bet for Horizon-Aware Anytime-Valid Testing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.19551)
*Ege Onur Taga,Samet Oymak,Shubhanshu Shekhar*

Main category: stat.ME

TL;DR: The paper develops a method for conducting horizon-aware tests and creating confidence sequences for bounded means, leveraging optimal control theory and reinforcement learning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The study seeks to improve statistical methods for limited-horizon scenarios, where decisions must be made under a strict deadline.

Method: The authors use betting/e-process framework, optimal control theory, and Deep Reinforcement Learning, particularly a Deep Q-Network (DQN), to optimize betting strategies across finite horizons.

Result: The proposed DQN policy achieves state-of-the-art performance in limited-horizon experiments by dynamically adapting bets based on statistical history.

Conclusion: The study provides a practical and theoretically grounded framework for horizon-aware testing, offering insights into optimal betting strategies and outperforming existing methods in experiments.

Abstract: We develop horizon-aware anytime-valid tests and confidence sequences for bounded means under a strict deadline $N$. Using the betting/e-process framework, we cast horizon-aware betting as a finite-horizon optimal control problem with state space $(t, \log W_t)$, where $t$ is the time and $W_t$ is the test martingale value. We first show that in certain interior regions of the state space, policies that deviate significantly from Kelly betting are provably suboptimal, while Kelly betting reaches the threshold with high probability. We then identify sufficient conditions showing that outside this region, more aggressive betting than Kelly can be better if the bettor is behind schedule, and less aggressive can be better if the bettor is ahead. Taken together these results suggest a simple phase diagram in the $(t, \log W_t)$ plane, delineating regions where Kelly, fractional Kelly, and aggressive betting may be preferable. Guided by this phase diagram, we introduce a Deep Reinforcement Learning approach based on a universal Deep Q-Network (DQN) agent that learns a single policy from synthetic experience and maps simple statistics of past observations to bets across horizons and null values. In limited-horizon experiments, the learned DQN policy yields state-of-the-art results.

</details>


<div id='eess.AS'></div>

# eess.AS [[Back]](#toc)

### [388] [Gesture2Speech: How Far Can Hand Movements Shape Expressive Speech?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.19831)
*Lokesh Kumar,Nirmesh Shah,Ashishkumar P. Gudmalwar,Pankaj Wasnik*

Main category: eess.AS

TL;DR: This paper introduces Gesture2Speech, a multimodal TTS system that uses hand gestures to dynamically influence speech prosody.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To explore the underinvestigated role of hand gestures in modulating prosody for naturalistic and expressive human-computer communication.

Method: The authors propose a Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architecture, combining linguistic and gesture features in a style extraction module. They introduce a gesture-speech alignment loss for synchronized gesture-prosody integration.

Result: Gesture2Speech achieves superior performance over existing baselines in both naturalness of speech and synchrony between gestures and prosody, as shown on the PATS dataset.

Conclusion: Gesture2Speech demonstrates the potential of integrating gesture cues in TTS systems, laying groundwork for future advances in multimodal neural speech synthesis systems.

Abstract: Human communication seamlessly integrates speech and bodily motion, where hand gestures naturally complement vocal prosody to express intent, emotion, and emphasis. While recent text-to-speech (TTS) systems have begun incorporating multimodal cues such as facial expressions or lip movements, the role of hand gestures in shaping prosody remains largely underexplored. We propose a novel multimodal TTS framework, Gesture2Speech, that leverages visual gesture cues to modulate prosody in synthesized speech. Motivated by the observation that confident and expressive speakers coordinate gestures with vocal prosody, we introduce a multimodal Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architecture that dynamically fuses linguistic content and gesture features within a dedicated style extraction module. The fused representation conditions an LLM-based speech decoder, enabling prosodic modulation that is temporally aligned with hand movements. We further design a gesture-speech alignment loss that explicitly models their temporal correspondence to ensure fine-grained synchrony between gestures and prosodic contours. Evaluations on the PATS dataset show that Gesture2Speech outperforms state-of-the-art baselines in both speech naturalness and gesture-speech synchrony. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first work to utilize hand gesture cues for prosody control in neural speech synthesis. Demo samples are available at https://research.sri-media-analysis.com/aaai26-beeu-gesture2speech/

</details>


<div id='cs.LO'></div>
